variables.name: has_lantern
variables.description: The companys brass lantern, won at Harrowstone.

variables.name: marsh_guide
variables.description: Reed the fen guide taught the party the marsh calls.

variables.name: choir_key
variables.description: The pale singers coral key, key to the drowned choir.

nodes.body: Barrowmere wears its three weeks of peace like a new coat, and the Brass Lantern sign creaks over the inn door with your companys name on it. Under the sign, nailed to the post this morning, hangs a marshals summons with Heddas seal pressed deep. The square hums with south road talk, pale strangers buying rope and salt, wagons that travel only at night. Bram is at his forge wagon, Sable is on a roof somewhere, Elowen is shelving simples, and the summons is addressed to all of you.

choices.label: Read the summons under the sign.
See to supplies in the market first.
Go straight in to the taproom.
Cross the square toward the well talk.
Stop at the roadside shrine a moment.

nodes.body: You read the summons twice under the creaking sign. Heddas hand is plain and short, come at once, paid work, bring the company. Below her seal someone has copied one line from the wizards burnt notes, the second sleeps beneath the Greymarsh. And under that, in chalk, a boys sketch of a coiled snake, the same mark you have been seeing on your maps for three weeks of bad dreams.

choices.label: Ask the gate watchman what he has seen.
Take the summons inside to the company table.
Decide the marsh is someone elses errand.

nodes.body: The gate watchman confirms all of it, leaning on his spear like a fence post. Pale robed buyers, soft spoken, paying in old coin. Wagons riding heavy toward the fords, always at dusk. And a marsh trader who left a deposit on stabling last month and never came back for his pony. The watchman spits and calls it fen business, and fen business, he says, never stays in the fen.

choices.label: Head in to the taproom with the news.

nodes.body: You decide the marsh is someone elses errand, and the summons stays nailed where it hangs. The road home is short and quiet, and for a week the decision even feels wise. Then the bells start being heard from the town walls at night, and the fen keeps its secrets a while longer, and keeps its prisoners too, and Barrowmere learns what the company might have been.

Market morning has learned your name. Stall keepers wave, prices bend, and a fishwife tells her boy loudly that these are the ones who shut the door under the hill. The south crossing has its own list though, and it is not signed by fame. Lamp oil for the brass lantern, rope that can hold a wet dwarf, and dry rations that will stay dry in a country made of water.

choices.label: Buy lamp oil for the brass lantern.
Buy eel line and a grapple.
Walk the stalls and listen.
Poke through the oddments cart.

nodes.body: The oil seller fills your jar with his best pressing and waves off half the price. For the heroes of Harrowstone, he says, loudly enough for three stalls to hear, and beams like a man buying a share in a story. You pay him the other half anyway. The brass lantern drinks light, Elowen says, and the fen is a long way from anywhere that sells more.

choices.label: Carry the oil jar to the inn.

nodes.body: The net makers widow measures out forty feet of eel line and a grapple that folds like a secret. The marsh takes rope, she says, the way winter takes candles, and she should know, her husband poled the Greymarsh twenty years. She ties the coil off with a carters hitch and will not be argued out of the discount.

choices.label: Carry the coil to the inn.

nodes.body: You walk the stalls slow and buy little, listening the way Sable taught you. Salt has doubled this month, and every buyer of it was a stranger in soft shoes. A tinker sold forty feet of chain to a man who never once asked the price. And the fishmongers all agree the eels are running strange, early and thin, like something downriver is eating first.

choices.label: Take what you heard to the inn.

nodes.body: The Brass Lantern taproom is empty this early, chairs still on tables, Pell sweeping yesterday out the door. Your table by the fire, the one where Hedda counted out fifty crowns, sits waiting like a small shrine nobody dares use. Pell nods at the summons in your hand, and knew it was coming, he says. Whole town knew before the nail was in.

choices.label: Size up the redcloak swords at the bar.
Go back to the company table.
Listen to the fiddlers new Harrowstone verse.

nodes.body: Two hired swords in red cloaks hold up the bar, loud and half gone in their cups before noon. They ride south tonight, they announce, to beat the famous company to the marsh bounty and teach the fen who real professionals are. One of them toasts your table without recognizing a single face at it, which is the best thing that has happened all morning.

choices.label: Race the redcloaks south this same hour.
Let them ride and plan it right.

nodes.body: You ride south the same hour to beat two braggarts, half provisioned and wholly unplanned. The fords take a horse shoe, the dusk takes the light, and a lamed horse by the marker stones takes the rest. The company limps home leading it, and the commission goes back to Heddas table unearned, with the redcloaks laughter following you up the road.

The taproom fills toward noon, and the noise of a town that believes in you is a strange warm weight. Pell tips his head toward the back, where the company table waits with the map space already cleared. Someone has chalked a small lantern on the wall above it, and nobody will say who.

choices.label: Join the table and spread the map.
Look in on the stable first.

nodes.body: The company table takes the summons flat in its middle, and the planning starts the way it always does, with everyone talking at once. The taproom around you is full of people who each know one useful thing about the fen. And full of ale that makes them generous with it. The crossing needs a route, the route needs the company whole, and two of the four have reasons to be talked back aboard.

choices.label: Talk the plan through with Pell.
Order supper and eat while you plan.
Help Pell shift the flooded cellar kegs.
Check the watchroom ledger yourself.
Ask the drovers about the south fords.
Hear Pips riddle about the marsh.
Let old Maren read the lantern flame.

nodes.body: Mutton and barley arrive without being ordered, and you plan over the steam while the taproom eats around you. Strength banked for the road, Bram would say. At the next table a braggart is telling Harrowstone all wrong, with himself in it, and the company listens to its own legend get taller while the gravy lasts.

choices.label: Bring Pell into the planning.
Correct the braggarts version loudly.

nodes.body: Pells cellar has flooded ankle deep with the spring rains, and he asks the favor sideways, the way proud men do. Kegs to shift onto staging before they spoil, an hours honest work in cold water. It is nothing like adventure and exactly like the fen, and by the last keg your back knows the difference between famous and useful.

choices.label: Shift the kegs the careful way.
Rush the job and wrench your back.

nodes.body: The duty sergeant slides the watchroom ledger across without being asked twice, and three south road reports in a month. A carter who heard bells over the mire where no church stands. A shepherd missing two dogs by the crow trees. And a fen family gone from their crannog between one market and the next, boat and all, door left open, porridge left warm.

choices.label: Take the reports back to Pell.

nodes.body: Pell leans close over the bar with a rag in his fist and the look of a man spending savings. The crossing needs Bram and Sable and Elowen back under one roof, he says, and two of the three have fresh reasons to say no. Bram is settling his debts like a dwarf leaving forever, and Sable has a letter from a southern fence that smells like real money. Start where you like, but start today. The fen will not wait on feelings.

choices.label: Start with Bram at his forge wagon.
Admit your own doubts first.
Study Heddas map scrap over the bar.
Hear Pells warning about the fen.

nodes.body: You say the doubt out loud, once, at the bar, because the marsh is not a keep. There are no walls to breach, no doors to bar behind you, only mud that keeps what it takes and water that does not care how famous you are. Pell polishes the same spot on the bar for a long moment. Then he says, quietly, that the fen folk in the pens have doubts too, and waits to see what your answer is.

choices.label: Square your shoulders and go find Bram.
Let the company rest on its one victory.

nodes.body: The doubt wins the bar argument, and the company stands down with its one victory kept safe like a coin too fine to spend. Lesser help goes south in your place, and some of it comes back. The Brass Lantern sign still creaks over the door, but the table by the fire goes back to being furniture, and the fen settles its own accounts.

Bram is at his forge wagon settling accounts like a dwarf leaving forever, shield new painted, tools wrapped in oiled cloth. He saw you coming from the town gate, he says, and started rehearsing no at the second lamppost. The marsh drowned better dwarves than him, his grandfather among them, and stone sense is worth nothing in a country with no stone. He keeps working while he talks, which means the door is not shut.

choices.label: Offer a full share and gate money.
Give him the plain truth of the taken.
Pick up the spare hammer and help first.
Stand him an ale and talk shield work.

nodes.body: You count a full share and gate money onto the anvil where he cannot pretend not to see it, coin by coin, unhurried. Bram watches the little stack grow with the expression of a man losing an argument with arithmetic. Gold is gold, he grumbles, but it does not float either. The stack sits there shining between you, patient as you are.

choices.label: Close the bargain on the anvil.
Push too hard when he hesitates.

nodes.body: You give him the plain truth instead, no varnish. The pale ones take fen folk the way the Redtooth took drovers, whole families poled into the dark, and a child is coughing behind bars somewhere south of the fords right now. The company is going with him or without him. Bram sets down his hammer very carefully, which from Bram is a shout.

choices.label: Wait out his long silence.
Push too hard when he hesitates.

nodes.body: You pick up his spare hammer and work an hour beside him without a word of the fen, finishing the wain hitch, truing a wheel band. Dwarves argue with silence better than most folk argue with words, and the work makes its own case. When the hitch is done he looks at it, then at you, and asks what time the company leaves.

choices.label: Ask him once, with the work done.
Push too hard when he hesitates.

nodes.body: You push the dwarf one sentence past his patience, and it snaps shut like a tool chest. Bram finishes his packing in silence, and the forge wagon rolls out at dawn without a word left behind for you. The company that crosses the fen without its wall learns quickly what the wall was for, and turns back before the deep mire, short one friendship and one shield.

The shield goes over the shoulder and the forge gets banked, and Bram states his one condition to the sky in general. No boats he cannot stand up in. He has seen your rowing, he adds, and would rather fight the marsh on foot like a civilized person. The company is one dwarf heavier by supper.

choices.label: Go find Sable on the roof ridge.

nodes.body: Sable is on the inn roof ridge, because of course she is, flipping a folded letter like a coin. A southern fence offers city work, clean and dry and paid in advance, she says before you are half up the ladder. She does not say she has decided. She lets the letter do its coin flip, over and over, while the street below pretends not to watch.

choices.label: Dice for it, letter against commission.
Read the fences letter at her shoulder.
Counter the offer, first pick and first name.
Bet her she cannot walk the ridge blind.

nodes.body: You dice for it on the ridge tiles, her letter against your commission, best of three under the whole towns windows. Sable cheats, obviously, and you know how she cheats, which makes it nearly fair. The bone dice rattle on the slates while the street holds its breath, and her laugh at the last throw tells the answer before the dice settle.

choices.label: Let the dice settle it clean.
Snatch the letter mid throw.

nodes.body: You read the letter at her shoulder instead of arguing. Fine paper, city hand, wages that make the commission look like pocket lint. Then the wax. The fences seal is pressed in gray, and the mark in it is a coiled snake. Sable goes very still, the way she does before knives happen, and the letter stops being an offer and becomes evidence.

choices.label: Point at the snake seal and say nothing.
Read the seal aloud to the street.

nodes.body: You name the snake seal out loud on a public roof, and the word carries the way words do when everyone below is already listening. Heads turn in the street. A watch sergeant starts toward the ladder with the walk of a man who owns it, and Sable breathes a curse at you that is also, somehow, agreement. The roof is suddenly a bad place to be famous.

choices.label: Talk fast and get off the roof.
Argue with the watch sergeant.

nodes.body: The roof argument ends with a watch sergeant and a plank bed, one night in the lockup for disturbing the kings peace. It is a friendly enough cell, as cells go, but the tide table does not care about friendly, and the seventh night is one night closer when they let you out. The company starts south late and short of temper, and the fen does not forgive schedules.

You make the counter offer once, plainly, and leave it to sit. First pick of anything the temple holds, and her name first on the company book, above yours. Sable studies you the way she studies locks, looking for the trick of it, and the letter stops flipping. That is all the answer she gives on the roof, and it is enough to wait on.

choices.label: Leave the offer to sit and turn away.
Snatch the letter to force the choice.

nodes.body: You snatch the letter mid throw, and the trust goes with it. Sable is over the ridgeline with her offer before your apology finishes, gone south alone by the smugglers roads to see the snake seal for herself. The company crosses the fen without its eyes, and finds out at the first patrol exactly how much that costs.

The letter folds into her boot, unanswered, which for Sable is a resignation delivered. The snake was hiring me away from the snake hunt, she says, and that decides it, nobody buys her off a job with the jobs own money. She is down the ladder before you are, already asking about the route.

choices.label: Turn in for one real night of sleep.

nodes.body: One full night in real beds at the Lantern, boots drying in a row by the fire like well fed dogs. The first whole sleep since Harrowstone comes down like a lid, and morning arrives with backs unknotted and tempers rinsed. Strength banked, as Bram says, spends best.

choices.label: Go to Elowen at her aunts shop.

nodes.body: Elowen is shelving simples in her aunts shop, and aunt Aldous is between her and the door with folded arms and a list of reasons old as guild law. The girl came back from the hills with nightmares, the aunt says, and shops do not run themselves. Elowen keeps shelving, eyes down, but the jars are going onto the shelves in the exact order of a field kit being packed.

choices.label: Vouch for her to aunt Aldous yourself.
Accept the aunts gathering errand.
Answer her wizard question honestly.
Ask Elowen what she wants, plainly.

nodes.body: You give Aldous your word across the counter, formal as a bond. The girl came home whole once and will again, and you will sign the shop ledger to it if she likes. The old woman produces the ledger so fast it must have been ready under the counter, and watches you sign with the eyes of a moneylender at a christening.

choices.label: Sign the shop ledger as her bond.
Bristle at the old womans terms.

nodes.body: You bristle at the old womans terms one degree too hot, and the shop door shuts with the bell still ringing. Elowen mouths sorry through the glass and keeps shelving. The company goes south without its scholar, and at the first warden mark nobody can read, the size of what stayed behind the counter becomes exactly clear.

Aldous names her price without blinking. Fen samphire, marsh mallow root, and three simples she names in the old tongue, gathered fresh on the crossing by her nieces own hand. A shop errand wrapped around a blessing, and both of them know it. Elowen copies the list twice, once for the kit and once for the lid of her chalk case.

choices.label: Take the list with good grace.
Take the list and the lecture both.

nodes.body: Elowen asks it straight, the way she asks everything. Is there a wizard in this one. And you answer honestly, worse, a whole choir of them, and something under the water that the wizards are working for. Her aunt hears every word from the doorway, which was the point of answering loudly. Elowen sets down the last jar and starts packing chalk.

choices.label: Promise her the truth the whole way.
Soften it, and let her hear the lie.

nodes.body: You soften the wizard answer into a kindness, and Elowen hears the lie under it the way she hears everything. She stays home on principle, politely, completely. Three at the ford where four were needed, and the fen finds the gap in the company before the first nightfall, the way water finds every gap there is.

The chalk case gets packed with two new sticks bought full price over her aunts protest. And Aldous nails her gathering list inside the lid like a charm against carelessness. At the door the old woman presses a twist of dried herbs into Elowens hand and says nothing at all, which in that shop is a long speech.

choices.label: Go to Hedda for the commission.

nodes.body: Hedda has the burnt notes spread on the marshals table and fifty crowns of new commission stacked on the corner, and she wastes no words on either. The Greymarsh circle is inked on her map over older, fainter lines. Find what the pale ones are digging for, break it or bring it home, and bring back her fen folk alive. The brass lantern sits between you on the table like a flag, and she looks at it a moment before she slides the crowns across.

choices.label: Set the brass lantern on the table and accept.
Accept the commission with a plain hand.
Ask for Tallows cart for the south leg.
Ask why the redcloaks were hired too.
Accept Heddas parting gift.

nodes.body: Tallows own freight cart stands ready at dawn, greased and provisioned, the merchants debt paid forward in axle and harness. His drover tips his hat and says the load bed is packed the way Tallow packs for people he intends to see again. The south road takes the wheels like an old acquaintance.

choices.label: Roll south for the fords at first light.

nodes.body: The South Fords is where the road stops pretending, and three ways run on toward the fen edge. The downs path climbs left through sheep country, high and dry and slow. Dead ahead the river landing rings its barge bell, and that is Wecks bell, no mistaking it. And right, the old drover droves sink between hedges into the carrlands, green tunnels pointing south. The cart stays stabled here either way, and from the fords on, the fen chooses what travels.

choices.label: Take the downs path over shepherd country.
Take passage on Wecks river barge.
Walk the drover droves through the carrlands.
Chase the dust plume on the downs first.
Work the weir walk shortcut past the landing.
Follow the stray bell note over the carrlands.

nodes.body: The downs path climbs through sheep runs toward a shepherds gate, and camped square across the stile sit two familiar red cloaks, demanding toll of everyone bound for the fen. They made it this far, then, and found their bounty easier to collect from travelers than from the marsh. A queue of one peddler is already arguing. The redcloaks look bored and dangerous in the way of men who have discovered extortion pays better than courage.

choices.label: Go the long way around the toll camp.
Face the redcloaks down at the stile.
Run the fallows shortcut past them.
Talk to the shepherd watching it all.
Chance the boggy cut below the stile.

nodes.body: You take the long way around the toll camp, up through the gorse line where the sheep tracks run. It is slow and legal and scratchy, and the redcloaks jeer at your backs the whole climb, delighted to have moved the famous company off its road. The gorse gives onto open down eventually, and the jeering thins into wind, which is better company anyway.

choices.label: Crest the downs by the gorse line.
Cut back to the shepherds lane.

nodes.body: You argue the toll down face to face, company reputation against redcloak bluster, and the whole stile becomes a theater. Their captain wants the win in front of his man more than he wants coin, and you can see him weighing how far to push it. Bram shifts his shield strap. The captain does arithmetic with his eyes and does not like the sum.

choices.label: Hold your ground until they blink.
Let Bram step forward and settle it.
Push past the captains hand.

nodes.body: The redcloak captain decides to push it all the way, and the stile becomes a brawl the company cannot afford to win or lose. The watch arrives from the fords at the worst moment, sorts nobody from nobody. And escorts everyone back down the road with the day and the tide lost. The toll camp reopens behind you, already telling the story its own way.

You run the fallows shortcut past the camp, and the dogs come loose behind you exactly as fast as you feared. The fallows are hummocked and treacherous at speed, a dry stone wall rising ahead, and the bite closing from behind. It is going to be close, and everyone knows it, including the dogs.

choices.label: Clear the wall and keep running.
Turn and face the dogs in the open.

nodes.body: The wall arrives a stride before the dogs and you clip it at speed, tumbling into the fallows in a tangle of kit and cursing. The toll camp collects four scraped travelers off the grass, docks the kit as damages with great ceremony. And sends the famous company back down the road while the dogs accept applause.

The downs crest at last, and the world falls away south into the Greymarsh, a tarnished mirror to the horizon, channels and meres and reed islands without end. Far out, a chapel bell tower stands drowned to its shoulders, marking where the land gave up. Somewhere past it, under all that shining water, is a temple and a family and a thing that sings. The path runs down to meet them.

choices.label: Take the shepherds lane down to the fen.
Study the drowned bell tower first.

nodes.body: The shepherds lane takes the far slope in easy loops, sheep bells and honest mud, gates that open and shut the way gates should. The first breath of marsh air comes up to meet you halfway down, salt and rot and patience, a smell with a memory in it now. The fen edge road shows below, thin as a drawn line.

choices.label: Follow the lane to the fen edge road.

nodes.body: You walk the run off on the downhill mile, scratched and winded and ahead of the toll camp by a margin that still counts. The dogs gave up at the wall, as dogs mostly do when the game stops being fun. The fen edge rises to meet the road, and your legs remember what they are for.

choices.label: Walk it off down to the fen edge.

nodes.body: Wecks eel barge sits at the landing stage like a patient brown dog, and the old man grins his gap grin at his famous passengers. Free passage for Harrowstone folk, he announces, then names a price anyway, company and news, paid in talk. The current runs south brown and fast with spring melt, and the barge takes it the way old boats do, creaking in a language only Weck speaks.

choices.label: Ride the barge and pump Weck for news.
Take poling lessons at the stern.
Help the eel boy with the pots.

nodes.body: The ride south is a whole watch of Wecks talk, and it is worth the fare twice over. Pale customers on the river this month, paying in old coin, riding his competitors barge because Weck quoted them triple out of pure dislike. They would not touch the water, he says, not to wash, not to drink. A river man notices what a man will not touch, and the eel pots bob past, and the fen edge slides closer.

choices.label: Swing the kit ashore at Saltmire stage.
Ride to the last bend for the long view.
Doze off to Wecks talk mid river.

nodes.body: You take poling lessons at the stern for the country ahead, and Weck corrects your grip with the patience of a man who has taught forty nephews. Push with the bottom, not your arms, walk the pole, love the mud. Blisters rise and get named after rivers. By the last reach you can hold a line against the current, which in the Greymarsh is the difference between traveling and drowning slowly.

choices.label: Land at the stage, blisters and all.
Pole the last reach yourself.
Ask Weck about the old charm he hums.

nodes.body: Saltmire stage arrives low and gray, half its planks newer than the rest, and the kit has to swing ashore over open water. The gap between gunwale and dock is wider than it looks and the water below is browner. Weck holds the barge as steady as current allows and offers no opinion, which is his way of offering one.

choices.label: Jump the gap with the kit balanced.
Toss the kit across first, carelessly.

nodes.body: The careless toss comes up short by a hand span, and the kit bag takes the brown water like it was born there. Lamp oil, dry rations, and the tide table all go down together, and the day goes after them, spent grappling the bottom while Weck offers proverbs. The trail south cools with every cast.

Weck takes the last bend slow to show you the country, and the drowned chapel off the port side, shoulder deep in its own parish. The crow trees, black with birds, where fen folk leave their dead season gifts. And south of all of it, under the horizon shine, the place he will not point at with his whole hand, only a thumb, the temple grounds. Then the stage, and the poles end.

choices.label: Put ashore at the crow trees.
Walk the bank while Weck ties up.

nodes.body: The drover droves run south between overgrown hedges, green lanes sunk below the fields by centuries of hooves. The cattle are long gone but the ruts are not, and the ruts are fresh. Heavy wheels riding low and southbound, yesterdays marks at the newest. Somebody is running cargo down the old droves where nobody watches, and the hedges keep their secrets both directions.

choices.label: Follow the heavy wheel marks.
Keep to the drove lane itself.
Cut across the open carrland sedge.
Count the wheel marks properly first.

nodes.body: You follow the wheel marks at a tracker walk, reading as you go. Heavy wagons, six or eight of them across three nights, riding lower going south than coming back. At a fork the ruts leave the drove for a black pool crossing, planks laid neat across the water, and the drove itself runs on toward the old ford. The ruts want following, and the planks want trusting, and neither wants both.

choices.label: Stay on the ruts to the ford.
Follow the ruts onto the black pool planks.

nodes.body: You keep the drove lane itself, and the hedges close overhead into a green tunnel, cool and dim and loud with wrens. Then the wrens stop. Something is pacing the far side of the blackthorn, big enough to push branches, patient enough to match your speed, and the hedge has exactly two gaps in the next mile.

choices.label: Push through to the old ford.
Slip through the hedge away from the pacing.

nodes.body: You cut across the open carrland to save the afternoon, and the ground starts honest and stops being honest a stride at a time. Firm, then springy, then shivering, the sedge nodding in circles around each bootfall. The fen edge is teaching its first lesson early, and the lesson is that flat is not the same as solid.

choices.label: Crawl the shivering ground flat and slow.
Sprint the last stretch before it opens.

nodes.body: The old ford waits where the drove meets the fen river, its marker stone leaning but honest, water at the knee and gravel underfoot. You cross with the kit high and the current polite. And the far bank takes you into fen country proper, where the road forgets it was ever a road.

choices.label: Cross at the marker stone.
Check the stones old carvings first.
Cross fast without sounding the water.

nodes.body: You try the black pool crossing where the wagon ruts went, and the planks are laid too neat, laid by someone who wanted followers. Three steps out the middle plank gives like bread under the crust, soft and wrong. And the pool underneath is blacker than pool water has any reason to be.

choices.label: Leap back off the soft plank.
Trust the plank one more step.

nodes.body: The plank gives way mid stride, and the black pool has no bottom that anyone finds. It was made of yesterdays wagon bait, laid for exactly this, and the pool keeps what it was set to catch. The wrens start singing again after a while, because the fen does not hold silences long.

The sedge cut finishes on hands and knees, soaked to the hips and cold to the bone, but the fen edge road shows ahead of dark. You wring out what wrings and count what the marsh took as tuition. Everyone walks lighter on the moss after that, which was the lesson.

choices.label: Wring out and make the fen edge road.

nodes.body: Hoofbeats come down the green tunnel behind you, unhurried, matched, wrong. Pale riders on marsh ponies, sweeping the droves the way a broom sweeps a hall, four abreast and in no hurry because the lane does their hurrying for them. The hedge offers one gap in the next fifty strides, a fox run through the blackthorn, low and dark. After that it offers wall, and the wall offers nothing.

choices.label: Vanish through the hedge gap and lie still.
Outrun the ponies on the straight.

nodes.body: The straight is lost before it starts. Marsh ponies are bred for the droves, and the green tunnel has no exits it did not already promise to the riders. They close in a hedge lane with walls both sides, polite and pale and certain, and the company rides south after all, bound and early and exactly where it was going.

Crows wheel over the fen edge road ahead, too many and too low, and under them a fen familys eel barge lies dragged ashore and broken open like a mussel. Catch and kit strewn in the mud, nets slashed, no bodies anywhere. And south from the wreck, printed deep in the tide mud, pole tracks going into the mire in a straight, unhurried line. The crows have opinions, and the mud has facts. Both are worth hearing.

choices.label: Search the broken barge itself.
Watch what the crows avoid upstream.
Read the pole tracks at the mud line.
Circle the wreck wide and watch first.
Walk the perimeter for dropped things.
Say fen words over the spilled catch.

nodes.body: You search the barge bow to stern, and the search tells a story you like less with every plank. The cargo was slit open and left, eels worth a months wages rotting in the bilge. Whoever did this wanted the family, not the catch. Freight thieves take freight. Something else takes people, and files them somewhere, and it filed this family south.

choices.label: Check the reed bank the crows shun.
Follow the pole tracks south now.
Search under the dragged hull.

nodes.body: You watch the crows instead of the wreck, the way Sable watches doors instead of rooms. They wheel wide of one reed bank upstream, every pass, a hole in their circling exactly the size of something they will not land near. Crows are honest that way. Whatever is in those reeds is alive, and the crows are waiting to find out for how long.

choices.label: Close on the reed bank quietly.
Throw a stone and see what rises.

nodes.body: You read the pole tracks at the mud line, and the mud gives its deposition. Six polers, pushing heavy, in step, which means discipline. Between their tracks, a drag line and one set of small boot prints, digging in, hauled forward, digging in again. Someone small fought the whole way to the water, and the tracks go south without a single wasted stride.

choices.label: Check the reed bank before leaving.
Follow the tracks south at once.
Cast wider for the small boots.

nodes.body: You circle the wreck wide first, patience spent like coin, reading the reeds for watchers before one boot enters the open. The circle turns up nothing but moorhens, a drag mark where a coracle went into the water, and the feeling, older than proof, that the wreck was left this visible on purpose. Bait or warning or both.

choices.label: Move in on the reed bank.

nodes.body: You close on the reed bank the crows shun, quiet feet on wet ground, and halfway in a low whistle answers your footfall. Two notes, rising, a marsh call, weak but human, from a burrow of bent reeds no bigger than a dog bed. Someone hid here when the taking happened, and stayed hidden, and is now deciding whether you are rescue or a second helping.

choices.label: Answer the two note call and go in.

nodes.body: You leave the road and follow the pole tracks straight south, fast and loud, kit slapping, and the mire swallows sound and bootprint alike. The tracks stay easy for a mile, which should have been the warning. Easy tracks in a fen mean the makers did not care who followed, and there are only two kinds of traveler who do not care.

choices.label: Push south hard along the pole trail.
Push on through the flooded reach.

nodes.body: The tracks vanish into a flooded reach, and you find them again the hard way, waist deep and shivering, casting along the far bank until frozen fingers find pole scars on a landing root. The trail lives. The margin between you and it has been paid in body heat, and the fen banks everything.

choices.label: Come out on the far bank and cast about.

nodes.body: The pole trail is followed loud and fast, and the fen files its report ahead of you. A picket waits where the reeds narrow, gray hoods rising on both sides at once, polite as ushers. The company is poled south exactly like the family it came to find, trussed and counted, and the one who counts sounds pleased with the arithmetic.

The reed burrow gives up a boy of maybe seventeen, arrow creased along one arm, half drowned and all fury. Reed, he says his name is, Wecks nephew, of the barge family. The one who slipped overboard when the pale ones came. He watched his mother and uncles poled south trussed like eels, and he has spent two days memorizing everything about the takers. Because somebody was going to come, and somebody was going to need it.

choices.label: Bind the wound and take his story.
Carry him until his legs come back.
Answer his challenge with the lantern.
Give him Elowens fen herbs first.
Send word back to Weck downriver.

nodes.body: You bind the arrow crease while Reed talks, and the story comes out straight as a pole line. Gray hoods, six of them, silent except one who counted. They poled his kin south toward the old temple grounds where nobody poles. Then he stops, looks at the company hard, and starts teaching you the marsh calls, heron, plover, all clear, because if you are going where his family went, you will need to sound like the fen.

choices.label: Learn the marsh calls and move.

nodes.body: Reeds legs give out a mile in, so you carry him on your own shoulders until they answer again. And he teaches the marsh calls breath by breath the whole way, heron for danger, plover for move, one long note for all clear. By the time he can walk, the whole company can talk like a wet spring morning, and Reed has decided you will do.

choices.label: Set him down at the hamlet path.

nodes.body: Reed asks it straight, who are you to go where fen folk drown. And the answer is the brass lantern, held up between you, and Harrowstone named once, quietly, without decoration. He looks at the lantern a long time. Fen folk heard about the door under the hill, he says at last. Everyone heard, and he is measuring whether the story is load bearing.

choices.label: Let him take the companys measure.
Tell the lantern story short and true.

nodes.body: The boy takes the companys measure one face at a time and gives his verdict fen fashion, with mud. Palms pressed, yours and his, swearing the company into fen kin, which obligates both directions, he is careful to say. Then the marsh calls, taught properly, because kin do not let kin sound like drowning tourists.

choices.label: Take the mud oath and the calls.

nodes.body: You tell the lantern story short and true, no varnish, including the parts where you were nearly too late. Reed nods at exactly the right places, which is how you know he has held a line while things went wrong. The calls get traded like fair freight after that, heron, plover, all clear, and the trade makes the company richer than coin would.

choices.label: Shoulder packs for Saltmire hamlet.

nodes.body: Saltmire hamlet is the last dry ground in the world, four crannogs and a smokehouse on stilts, and its fen wives feed the company like a returning tide while the crossing gets argued. The headwoman knows four ways into the deep mire. The old causeway, drowned but walkable, and the esker road, high and watched. The grange sluice, dark and dry. And the peat galleries, under everything. None of them kind, she says, but the fen was never kind, only fair.

choices.label: Take the headwomans floor for the night.
Go over every strap and buckle.
Argue for beating the storm south.
Gather the hamlets fen lore.
Climb the old burial mound for the view.
Trade news with the peat cutters.

nodes.body: You take the headwomans floor for the night, dry peat smoke and borrowed blankets and the rain arguing with the thatch. The fen wives mend two cloaks nobody asked them to mend. Sleep comes down like a tide, and morning finds the company rested for the first time since the fords, strength banked where the fen cannot tax it.

choices.label: Start clean in the morning mist.
Oversleep and rush the start.
Hear the headwomans last counsel.

nodes.body: The early start goes clean, kit squared while the mist is still down on the meres, the hamlet turned out to wave the company off the dry ground. The headwoman presses smoked eel on everyone with the force of law. The pole trail takes the first boots at dawn exactly, which Reed says the fen notices and respects.

choices.label: Take the marked pole trail south.

nodes.body: The late start goes the other way, kit half packed in a scramble. One cook pot left sitting on the crannog boards in plain sight of everyone pretending not to see it. The sun burns the mist off the open mire before the first mile is done, and the day runs hot and public, which is not how Reed likes his fen.

choices.label: Take the trail and make up time.

nodes.body: You go over the gear piece by piece on the hamlet common, straps waxed, the lantern trimmed and its spare wick dried, every buckle tied off against the mud. The fen wives watch with professional interest and correct two knots without asking. When it is done, the kit is fen ready, and the fen wives say so, which is the only inspection that counts here.

choices.label: Set out with the kit squared away.
Water the ponies at the old corral first.

nodes.body: The storm argument runs the whole supper. Black weather is stacking in the south, and the headwoman advises one more night on the dry ground, while the tide table burns red in your pocket, seven nights and counting. Wait and lose a day, and go and race the sky, and the fire spits, the thatch hums, and the company looks at you.

choices.label: Go now and race the black weather.
Push into the storm regardless.

nodes.body: You race the black weather and the black weather wins, the way weather does. The storm pins the company on a shrinking hummock through a long screaming night, kit soaking, no fire possible, and dawn spends itself limping back to Saltmire to start again poorer. The headwoman says nothing at all, which is worse than anything she could say.

You gather the hamlets fen lore by the fire, and it comes in proverbs. Which meres lie, which sing, why nobody poles within earshot of the bells. The headwoman saves the best for last, her grandmothers rule about the temple grounds, delivered flat as a tide table, never at floodtide. Nobody at the fire will say what happens at floodtide. That silence is the lore.

choices.label: Set out wiser in the morning.
Sit with the map over the fire first.

nodes.body: The pole trail leaves the dry ground at dawn, marked with rag knots on bent alders, fen writing for those who can read it. The mire opens flat and shining and wrong to the horizon, sky above and sky below and a thin lane of moss between. Reed walks first with his pole sounding, and the company walks like the fen is listening, because it is.

choices.label: Follow the rag knots at a fen pace.
Let Reed sound the lane ahead.
Check the burned polers platform.
Send a decoy splash down a side channel.
Push on into the dark to gain a march.

nodes.body: The rag knot line runs fast and wet between two meres, and one stretch dips under standing water, knee deep for forty strides. The knots resume on the far side, honest as signposts. Wade it on the line and trust the fen wives knots, or hunt a dry way around and trust your own eye, and the water does not care which, and says so.

choices.label: Wade the flooded dip on the line.
Guess a shortcut off the rag line.

nodes.body: The shortcut off the rag line lasts eleven strides. The twelfth finds moss that was never ground at all, just water wearing a green coat, and the lane drowns its guesser quietly, without hurry, while the marked trail looks on from forty feet away. The fen wives knots were honest. They usually are.

You take the lane slow behind Reeds pole, sounding every stride, and the mire breathes under the moss like a sleeping herd, rising a finger, falling a finger. Reed names the meres as they pass, the liar, the singer, the one with no name because naming it invites it. Slow is fast in the fen, he says, and proves it by sunset.

choices.label: Keep the slow pace to the crannog.
Pause at the singing mere Reed names.

nodes.body: A polers rest platform stands on stilts ahead, half burned, Coil leavings scattered on its boards, rope ends and a cold fire and one gray rag. And from here, for the first time, you hear it with your own ears. One low bell note, far south, held and held and held, never quite ending. The seal, already half singing, and everyone stands still until it fades, and it does not fade.

choices.label: Push on toward the crannog smoke.
Rest cold at the burned platform.
Search the Coil leavings for papers.

nodes.body: You pass the platform the long way around its open water, cold wading with eyes on the reeds the whole circuit, because burned platforms keep watchers the way carcasses keep crows. Nothing rises. The trail takes you back at the cost of wet legs and an hour, both spent on the principle that cheap lessons are the good ones.

choices.label: Wade on to the crannog landing.

nodes.body: The crannog at the trails end holds one fen boy of ten and one gray muzzled dog, both of them keeping watch like it is a paid position. Col, the boy says, of the barge family, left with the dog when the pale ones came because someone had to hold the crannog. He saw them pole his mother and uncles south toward the drowned temple. And he points the way with a hand that does not shake, because someone must, and everyone bigger is already taken.

choices.label: Follow where Col points, into the deep mire.

nodes.body: The deep mire at last, and the temples bell tower breaks the horizon like a rotten tooth, closer than it has any right to look. Col pointed true, and four ways run in from here. The drowned causeway, stones awash but walkable, straight as a ruled line. The esker road, high gravel ridges and stilt bridges, dry and watched. The old grange sluice, a dark dry drain under everything. And the peat cutters galleries, under even that. The bell note hangs over all four like weather.

choices.label: Take the drowned causeway stones.
Take the high esker road and bridges.
Light the lantern and take the grange sluice.
Go under the moss by the peat galleries.

nodes.body: The causeway runs arrow straight through open water, stones awash at every dip, and it is not empty. Coil poler patrols work its length in pairs, gray hoods swinging lanterns, passing each other with signs you cannot read yet. Cover exists only where the old parapet still stands, waist high stretches of it, gapped like bad teeth. The temple sits at the far end of all of it, patient as arithmetic.

choices.label: Bluff the patrols in gray hoods.
Time the sweeps and wade the gaps.
Wade the flanking shallows instead.
Study the parapet line for cover first.
Check under the causeway arches.
Listen to the patrol horns pattern.

nodes.body: A poler pair comes head on at a narrow with no cover either way, and Sables trick is ready, two gray rags worn hood fashion, heads down, poles shouldered. Walk like you belong or fight like you do not, she breathes, and there is no third road. The pair closes, lantern swinging, close enough now to smell the bilge on them.

choices.label: Walk the narrow like you belong.
Take the pair down quiet instead.
Overplay the bluff with a Coil sign.

nodes.body: You time the patrol from a parapet gap, and the pattern gives itself up. Twelve breaths between sweeps, regular as a tide clock, with a flooded stretch to wade inside that window and a watch horn hanging over the whole business on a post. Twelve breaths is enough. Barely, wetly, but enough, if nobody slips and nothing sings.

choices.label: Wade the stretch inside the window.
Borrow the supply skiff at its mooring.
Cut the horn down first, loudly wrong.
Check the chain locker under the stones.

nodes.body: You leave the causeway for the flanking shallows, wading the soft margin below the stones where the patrols never look, because nobody sane walks there. It is slower and wetter and the mud pulls like a debt, but the eye line from the stones passes clean over your heads. And the fen keeps its trespassers secret out of habit.

choices.label: Slog the margin the whole way.
Cut across the black lagoon.
Rest on the dry hummock first.

nodes.body: The bluff holds all the way past the narrow. Two gray nods traded, poles tipped, and the pair sweeps on without a backward glance, because the Coil hires widely and nobody knows every face. The causeway spends its last dry mile toward the terrace ring wall, and you spend it walking like you have walked it a hundred times.

choices.label: Walk the last mile to the ring wall.

nodes.body: You take the fight instead, short and quiet behind the parapet. Bram folds one poler over his own pole, Sable convinces the other, and both go into the reeds with their horn unspent and their hoods missing. The last mile walks easier in borrowed gray, though Elowen keeps pulling her hood down like it argues with her.

choices.label: Take the mile in borrowed hoods.

nodes.body: You add a Coil sign to the bluff, one flourish too many, and the sign has a countersign you never learned. The pairs lantern comes up. The narrow fills from both ends with gray hoods while the bluff dies on your hands, and the causeway walks its catch to the temple in good order, poles shouldered, lesson delivered.

The window works. Twelve breaths, a wet scramble through the flooded stretch, and the far parapet takes you in with two breaths to spare while the sweep passes behind. Cold to the waist, heart loud, but unseen, and the ring wall rises ahead at the causeways end like a promise being kept.

choices.label: Gain the far parapet and push on.

nodes.body: The patrols own supply skiff sits at its mooring, half loaded, unwatched, and you borrow it the way Sable borrows things, completely. Poled casual as freight down the last mile, hoods down, cargo piled to look like cargo. Gray hoods glance from the stones and see the most ordinary thing on the water. The water gate itself opens the view at the end.

choices.label: Pole the skiff to the water gate.

nodes.body: The shallows margin takes the whole distance, mud to the thigh and every stride bought with strength you will want later. But the ring wall arrives unremarked, its broken stretch exactly where the headwoman promised. And nobody on the stones ever looked down. The fen taxes the body and pays out in silence.

choices.label: Come up on the ring wall unremarked.

nodes.body: The margin shortcut crosses a black bottomed lagoon, and Reeds pole finds no bottom at all, just cold that climbs the shaft into his hands. The choice stands plain, the long slog back around, or the swim, thirty strides of water the color of a closed eye. The tide table burns in your pocket. The lagoon waits, flat as a held breath.

choices.label: Slog back and take the long margin.
Swim the black lagoon for the hour saved.

nodes.body: The swim is half won when the cold cramps close, and the pull from below is not current, whatever it is. The black lagoon smooths itself over the hour it saved, and the company hauls out short one swimmer and long one grief. And the causeway patrols never even hear the splashing.

The horn comes down loud and wrong, and the causeway wakes end to end like a struck fence wire. The long splashing retreat off the stones runs a full mile with poles beating the reeds behind. And dry ground takes you back at dusk, hunted, soaked, and no closer to the temple than the day you left home.

The esker road stands over the mire like the spine of some drowned animal. High gravel ridges chained together by stilt bridges, wind and gulls and long sight lines all day. The temple tower rides dead ahead the whole march, never nearer, never farther, the way mountains behave. Dry boots and open sky, and the constant knowledge that anything with eyes can count you against the horizon.

choices.label: Cross the broken stilt bridge first.
Scramble the collapsed scarp with the gear.
Round the scarp by the gull ledge.
Take the old pilgrim stair marked on the map.
Wait for dusk and walk the crest dark.
Glass the whole road from the first ridge.

nodes.body: The first stilt bridge is missing three spans, a gap of brown water where last winters ice took its toll. The fen offers plank scraps, rotten rope, and one dead alder for kit. Bram studies the gap like an engineering insult, and Sable studies it like a dare. Elowen measures it in chalk lengths and says nothing encouraging.

choices.label: Lash planks and bridge the gap.
Climb down and around the broken span.
Turn back and pick another road.
Check the old beacon bracket above the scarp.

nodes.body: The second esker scarp has collapsed to a scramble of running gravel, coins of stone sliding underfoot at every step. The gear can go up roped in stages, slow and sure, or on backs in one loaded push. The scarp does not care. It will run the same either way, and the gravel keeps a tally nobody sees until it calls it in.

choices.label: Scramble it loaded and save the hour.
Rope the gear up in stages.
Toss the packs up the scarp first.

nodes.body: A ledge path rounds the scarp face through gull country, nests in every socket, guano frosting the holds. And pressed in the softest of it, fresh this morning, one boot print that is not the companys. Somebody walks this road ahead of you, somebody with small feet and no interest in being seen. The gulls scream about everything, which helps nobody.

choices.label: Follow the boot print owner slow.
Take the ledge quick past the nests.
Edge past the gull colony at a hush.

nodes.body: The gap gets bridged with planks and eel line, Brams lashings holding with a carpenters grace, and the company crosses one at a time over the brown water while the spans creak philosophy. The last one over pulls the planks after, on principle. The esker road runs on, and the bridge is a story now, not a door.

choices.label: Cross last and pull the planks after.

nodes.body: The long way down and around the broken span costs mud and patience in equal measure, down the esker flank, across the soft, up again on hands and knees. The sun spends fast while you spend slower. And the road regains its ridge beyond the gap with the afternoon half gone and the temple tower exactly where it was.

choices.label: Regain the esker crest beyond.

nodes.body: The scarp scramble goes loaded, and the gravel runs like spilled coins under every boot. One bad slide gets arrested on the dead alder halfway down, breath and skin both paid, and the crest arrives at last under burning legs. The kit made it. The margin did not, and the day owes nobody a refund.

choices.label: Climb on to the crest, breath rationed.
Ride the gravel slide to save time.

nodes.body: The gravel slide gets ridden for the minute it saves, and the dead alder passes at speed, just out of reach. The scarp foot arrives before any plan does. The esker road is finished with you, and the gulls, who watched the whole lesson, go back to their nests unimpressed.

The esker crest walk lays the marsh out both sides like a map of itself, and ahead, exactly where the crest narrows, a watch platform stands on stilts with gray cloth snapping from its rail. One sentry, maybe two. The path passes directly beneath it, because of course it does. The platform owns the crest the way a toll booth owns a bridge, and the wind carries sound both ways.

choices.label: Pass the platform on its blind side.
Rush the ladder and bundle the sentry.
Wait below for the watch change.

nodes.body: You pass on the platforms blind side at a crouch, under the floor slats themselves, while a bored sentry hums a wage song above your heads. Dust sifts through the boards with his boot taps. Ten strides of held breath, then open crest again, and the temple terrace in plain sight beyond, close enough now to count its watch fires.

choices.label: Crouch on under the floor slats.

nodes.body: You rush the ladder instead, up and over the rail before the hum finishes its verse. One sentry, bundled into his own gray cloak with Brams hand for a gag, trussed and tucked behind the rail with great professionalism. The watch fire stays lit, innocent as morning, and the platform keeps lying to the temple for the rest of the night on your behalf.

choices.label: Leave the fire burning and move.

nodes.body: You wait below the scarp for the watch change, cold and cramped in gravel shadow, and read the relief pattern for the toll it is. Two sentries walk north at the change, two walk south, and for two hundred breaths the platform stands empty as a promise. Patience is the cheapest coin the fen accepts, and it accepts it grudgingly.

choices.label: Use the change and walk through it.
Cut straight down the scarp now.

nodes.body: The change comes exactly as read, and the company walks south under the platform in the same minute the relief walks north. Two lines passing in the dark with one story between them. The terrace ring wall rises ahead, broken and manned and singing faintly. And the esker road delivers you to it dry shod, which is more than any other road promised.

choices.label: Walk south under the relief minute.

nodes.body: The boot prints owner is met halfway around the ledge, and he brought friends. Gull cries cover the scuffle poorly and the numbers worse. The watch platform gains four guests roped in a line, marched crest to terrace with the whole marsh watching, and the temple takes delivery without surprise.

The crest wind rises through the afternoon to an open handed push, and the last spans sway like rigging in a gale. The road itself is saying no. The company backs off the heights a day poorer and starts the mire again from the bottom, and the wind spends the night laughing along the ridge line.

The old grange sluice opens in an alder brake like a secret kept by trees, a brick drain tall as Bram running dry and dark under the mire, straight toward the temple grounds. Abbey monks built it to drain fields the fen took back anyway, and their brick has outlived their names. The dark inside is total, patient, and exactly what the brass lantern was bought for. Time for it to earn its keep.

choices.label: Walk the dry run by lantern light.
Feel the dark run to save the oil.
Crawl the side drain past the flood.
Tap the walls for the hollow line.
Check the old sluice dam machinery.
Read the grange marks at the mouth.

nodes.body: The sluice walks dry by lantern light, brick courses sound as the day the grange masons laid them, the flame steady in dead air. At every junction old warden marks sit scratched at shoulder height, and they all point one way, down. Elowen copies each one into her chalk case. Somebody walked this drain before the fen rose, and left directions for whoever came after. You are whoever came after.

choices.label: Follow the warden marks down.
Map the junctions as you go.
Copy the tally scratched by the door.

nodes.body: You try the sluice dark to save the oil, hands on the crown course, counting junctions by touch. The dark is total and full of small sounds that stop when you stop, resume when you resume, always a half beat behind, like the drain learning to echo. The count matters now, and the count is the only map there is.

choices.label: Wade the flooded run in the dark.
Walk the eel run slow and loose.
Whistle to test the echo ahead.
Strike a light for just one look.

nodes.body: The side drain takes the flooded stretchs place, half height and half flooded itself, a crawl with the kit floated ahead on a plank. Elbows in cold water, back scraping brick, the lantern hooded to a coin of light on the plank ahead. It is slow as penance and twice as instructive, and it does move south, which is all anyone asks of it.

choices.label: Rejoin the flooded main run.
Push the unmapped forks on the count.

nodes.body: The grange cistern opens overhead like a brick sky, a vaulted chamber where all the drains meet, and an iron ladder climbs its wall to a grating with daylight standing in bars above. Terrace daylight. The temples own outer ground, reached from underneath, the way the wardens meant it to be reached. The lantern gets hooded. The ladder gets climbed.

choices.label: Climb the iron ladder to the grate.

nodes.body: A junction chamber offers three mouths, and the warden marks get read again by lantern light, careful as scripture. One mouth breathes cold air with a mineral edge, temple air, stone that has never dried. The count says the same mouth the marks say, and when map and mud agree, Reed says, go, and you go.

choices.label: Take the mouth that breathes cold.

nodes.body: The flooded main run wades chest deep, lantern held high, and the water pushes back the way water never should in a dead drain, a slow pulse, like breath. Something below moves it, far down the line, rhythmic and patient. Nobody names it. Everybody times their strides to the pulse, between pushes, and the cistern ladder cannot come soon enough.

choices.label: Push through to the cistern ladder.
Force the screeching grate ahead.

nodes.body: The dark stretch turns out to be an eel run, bodies thick as cables against your legs, a river of muscle flowing the other way. Reed gives the rule through his teeth, walk slow, never grab. The eels part around boots like water around stones, uninterested, uncountable, and the far end of the dark arrives one unhurried stride at a time.

choices.label: Walk it slow to the far side.
Kick free when the cables tighten.

nodes.body: The side drain forks twice where no fork was counted, and the plank kit snags on brick at the worst width. Then the count itself splits, your tally saying second left, Sables saying first, both said with the confidence of people who counted in the dark. The drain waits, and the water creeps, and one count is right.

choices.label: Trust Sables count over yours.
Press on by your own reckoning.

nodes.body: The seized grate ahead gets forced, and the screech runs the whole drain like wire pulled through a fiddle. Boots converge on the cistern grating above before the echo dies, and the ladder climbs into a ring of leveled poles. The drain was a good road, and it ends the way good roads do when hurried, at a toll booth.

The cables tighten and the rule breaks, one grab against the current of bodies, and the run answers the grab the way rivers answer dams. The drain keeps its rule and its taker both under the black water, and the eels flow on south. Uncounted, uninterested, exactly as old as the rule they enforce.

Your own reckoning wins the argument and loses the drain. The forks multiply past any count, the oil spends itself in circles of identical brick. And the side drains finally surrender the company back to daylight a full day behind and a mile off line. Blinking at a sun that owes nobody an apology.

The peat cutters galleries open under the moss like a house under a carpet, dry black tunnels propped with alder, cut by Saltmire hands across five generations. Reed knows the first mile blindfold. Past that, he says, the props change, because somebody has been paying for a straight run toward the temple, fast work, hired work. The old galleries wander like gossip. The new cut runs like a debt.

choices.label: Rest at the warm kiln chamber.
Deal with the hired dig crew ahead.
Thread the old workings around them.
Read the kilns for who fired them.
Sound out the rival cutters first.
Follow the newest prop line.

nodes.body: A cutters rest chamber opens off the gallery, char kiln still warm, dry peat beds stacked square, air clean as any cellar. The hills own pantry, Reed calls it, and the company steals an hour of rest in it, boots off, backs flat, the lantern turned low. The fen taxes everything, but the galleries give a little back.

choices.label: Take the straight run to the brick face.
Read the kiln tenders tally stick first.

nodes.body: Voices carry down the gallery ahead, work voices, a hired dig crew of strangers cutting by tallow light where the new run drives toward the temple. Not Coil robes, just paid hands, but paid hands count heads and collect bounties. Bluff past them, detour around them, or go deeper into the old unpropped cuts. All three roads smell of tallow and risk.

choices.label: Pass the crew as hired diggers.
Detour by the sweet aired old cuts.
Bluff with a foremans bark.
Take the deep unpropped cut around.
Listen to the diggers work song first.

nodes.body: The old workings bend around the crew the long way, props gray with age, roof brushing hair. In the low stretches the air goes sweet and heavy, marsh gas pooling where the roof dips, and Elowen watches the lantern flame like a book, reading its color, calling the safe breaths. Sweet air sings you to sleep, Reed says, and do not listen.

choices.label: Thread the old cuts on Elowens chalk.
Squeeze the roof fall gap ahead.
Hurry the low sweet stretch.

nodes.body: The straight run beyond the rest chamber is new work, fresh props, fresh spoil, floor tramped hard by paid boots. It ends at a brick face that stops everything, the temples own foundation, older than the galleries, older than the grange. And a cut has already been begun in it, bricks pried out chest high, work paused between shifts. The Coil is digging into its own temple, from below, in secret.

choices.label: Come up the cutters shaft at the wall.

nodes.body: The dig crews tally shack stands in the dark, unmanned between shifts, wage slates racked and a route board chalked wall wide. The whole hired operation reads at a glance, shifts, galleries, and one line that matters, terrace grate, third bell, keep clear for the divers. Elowen copies. Sable pockets a wage slate. Knowledge is the only pay this shack ever handed out honestly.

choices.label: Slip past to the terrace grate ladder.
Wade the sprung gallery beyond.
Linger copying the route board.
Pocket the wage slates for proof.

nodes.body: The last gallery is flooded to the knee where the dig broke a spring, cold water in dry country, props groaning about it. The wade goes slow with the lantern high, and the terrace grate ladder finally shows in the hooded light, iron rungs going up into stone. Above, faint through the grating, torchlight moves, and the bell note is suddenly very near.

choices.label: Climb the grate ladder to the terrace.

nodes.body: The old workings thread clean around the whole hired operation, Elowens chalk marking every turn taken. And the last cutters shaft climbs into open dusk inside sight of the ring wall. Behind, under the moss, the diggers work on for wages, none the wiser. The company counts its blessings and its chalk sticks, both diminished, both sufficient.

choices.label: Come up inside sight of the ring wall.

nodes.body: A roof fall blocks the old working ahead, spoil to the ceiling except one gap at the top, a body wide, and the spoil is still trickling, small stones ticking down like a clock deciding. The hill is not finished falling here. The gap will pass one careful body at a time, or it will not, and the trickle keeps its own counsel.

choices.label: Squeeze the gap while it holds.
Dig the gap wider first.

nodes.body: The gap gets dug wider against the trickle, and the roof takes the widening as permission. The old working shuts like a book behind a wall of falling spoil, and the company claws back to air through the dust with the route gone and the night spent, coughing black and starting over. The hill said keep it. The hill keeps it.

The low stretch gets hurried, and the sweet air wins at the dip, soft as a lullaby. The waking comes on the spoil heap outside with aching heads and no memory of the carry, the rival cutters standing over you with the bill. Hauled out by strangers, Reed says later, is the politest thing the galleries ever do to the careless.

The foremans bark gets answered by the real foreman, and the lantern count comes up one crew short and four strangers long. The diggers are paid hands, and the Coil pays a standing bounty for exactly this. The galleries end at the temple after all, at pole point, with the wage slates balancing perfectly for everyone but you.

The temples outer terrace spreads before you at dusk tide, ring wall broken but manned, watch fires burning at the corners. A marsh hound pen crowds the water gate, and a reed fodder sledge is being hauled in dripping by two grumbling Coil hands. Along the east wall a barred pen grate glows faint with rush light, shapes moving behind it. The fen folk, alive, close enough that you can hear a child coughing. The sound rearranges everything in your chest.

choices.label: Work the wall line to the pen grate.
Burrow into the wet reed sledge.
Take the sentry platform quietly.
Sound Reeds all clear and walk across.
Chance the open pavement at a sprint.
Slip through the old shell midden yard.
Watch the water gate lamp a while first.

nodes.body: You work the wall line shadow to shadow, and at the pen grate a fen wifes eyes meet yours and go wide as cart wheels. You put a finger to your lips. Hope travels through the iron both directions, silent and enormous. Then comes the hound pen, and a dozen wet noses swing up together to read the wind, and the wind, unhelpfully, is behind you.

choices.label: Slip the shell midden door in the angle.
Work the bar of the water gate side door.
Hurry past the hound pen upwind.

nodes.body: You burrow into the wet reed sledge and hold your breath while two Coil hands haul it grumbling toward the inner gate. Their complaint is the flood tide timetable, floodtide floodtide floodtide, like a curse with a calendar. The sledge drips and sways with the party buried in it like winter eels, and through the reeds you watch the inner arch swallow the sky.

choices.label: Cross by the sledges shadow to the stair.
Slip the midden door as the sledge halts.
Ride the sledge all the way to the kennels.

nodes.body: You take the sentry platform tread by soft tread, and the one gray hood above is yawning at the first stars when Sable reaches him. Her work is quick and quiet as folding cloth. A gray cloak and a shell horn become trophies, the platform becomes yours until the relief, and the whole terrace lies below, fires and circuits and the long dark line of the ring wall, waiting to be read.

choices.label: Work the side doors bar from above.
Read the whole terrace before moving.
Sound the sentrys own horn for cover.
Check the old well head by the platform.

nodes.body: You put Reeds lessons to work and sound the all clear, one long heron note, true as the fen itself. The corner fires ease at the sound of their own signal. Then you cross the terrace at a polers trudge, hood down, heart loud enough to wake the hounds on its own, and gray eyes slide over the company like rain off wax. The longest short walk since Harrowstone, and the inner gate arrives like a held breath released.

choices.label: Cross to the descending stair unremarked.

nodes.body: The shell midden door sits in the wall angle, unbarred, because nobody guards a smell. A century of oyster shell and fish bone shifts underfoot like coins as the party slips through one at a time. Beyond, a service stair goes down into the temples cold, salt dark rising off it like the breath of a well, and the bell note waits at the bottom.

choices.label: Take the service stair down.

nodes.body: The water gates side door stands barred from within, but the bar throws its shadow through the gap between door and frame. Sables knife slides into that gap and walks the bar up out of its brackets a finger width at a time, patient as frost heaving a stone. It comes free without one sound, and the temples own dark invites you in.

choices.label: Ease the door and enter the dark.

nodes.body: You cross the inner yard in the sledges dripping shadow, letting the unloading noise cover your boots, and gulp a doorway one heartbeat after a torch passes it. Inside, the descending stair waits, cold and quiet, coiling down into the temple. Somewhere below, faint, the child coughs again, and the sound pulls the whole company downward like a hand on a rope.

choices.label: Start down the descending stair.

nodes.body: You read the terrace from the platform first, and it reads like a page. Fire circuits counted, twelve breaths per round, and the pen grates guard change marked against the torch by the gate. The sledge path chalked in memory, arch to arch, and the side doors bar glinting through its gap, liftable by a patient knife. Knowledge worth the delay, all of it, and the terrace none the wiser for being read.

choices.label: Go down by the route you chalked.
Read the old marks around the arch first.

nodes.body: One nose wins its argument with the wind, and the hound pen goes up like a kicked hive. Torches converge from three corners at once, and there is nothing for it but the ring wall breach, retaken a stride ahead of the pack with the hunt giving tongue behind you. The mire chases you to dry ground empty handed, and the last thing you hear from the temple is the hounds, singing their triumph to the tide.

The open pavement is going well until a relief guard arrives early to his post, and stops, and looks. The shout and the rush arrive together, and on bare stone there is nowhere to be anything but caught. The pen gains four new tenants who came to empty it, and the fen wifes face through the grating, watching you marched past, is the worst part of a bad night.

The heron call cracks on a dry lip into something no marsh bird ever said. The whole terrace answers it on its feet, fires flaring, hounds boiling, and there is nothing to do but run the ring wall back the way you came with the hunt pouring up behind. The breach takes you out a pole length ahead of the consequence, and the mire hunts you home.

Inside the sunken temple at last. The nave stands drowned, tide water black and still in the aisles, and a stair coils down into the flooded dark with fresh torch soot streaking its vault. The childs cough drifts up again, below and to the east now, a small stubborn sound in all that stone. And under everything, felt through the soles of your boots, one low bell note, held and held and held. The second seal, already half singing, and you start down toward it.

choices.label: Start down the coiling stair toward the bell.

nodes.body: The stair opens on the temples mid level, and three ways run from the landing. East, a working passage smells of brine and rush light, and the cough lives down there somewhere, behind iron. Ahead, the great refectory hall booms with the Coil feeding between tides, benches and torchlight and a hundred voices. And to the west a grotto passage breathes incense and one thin thread of song, the same song the seal hums, carried by a human throat. Three doors into the machine.

choices.label: Go east toward the pens and the cough.
Scout the great refectory hall.
Follow the thread of song to the grotto.

nodes.body: The war court spreads below the choir loft lattice, and you press an eye to the carved gap. Korvane the Coil master paces the floor with a lead bound folio chained to his belt, explaining in a voice like cold water rising. Below him the seal font sits on its raising cradle, chains run to the ceiling gear. Gorse of the hired Reedcloaks stands apart with his honor guard and asks the only question with teeth in it. Serve whom. Every bell in the deep answers nothing.

choices.label: Stay for the whole plan, every word.
Mark the relay rotations instead.
Study Korvane himself, cold.
Shift for a better view through the lattice.
Read the ledger open on his camp desk.

nodes.body: You stay, and the whole plan unfolds. At floodtide the font rises to the drowned door. The Whisper sings the seal awake, Korvane speaks the waking words from his folio, and the king under the water rises bound to the voice that woke him. The Reedcloaks are to be paid in dead mens silver, and in the taken fen folk. Gorse spits at that last, saying prisoners were never the bargain, and the court goes knife quiet before Korvane smiles it smooth again. You have everything now except time.

choices.label: Slip down as the court breaks up.
Watch Gorses face one moment more.
Stay for one last sentence.

nodes.body: You mark the rotations from the lattice instead of the words. Torch relays are already flowing down the processional stair by pairs, the court emptying itself rank by rank toward the deep. Circuits, timings, the gaps between, all of it writes itself into memory like a stolen tide table. Whatever happens at the door tonight, you now know exactly how the temple will move around it.

choices.label: Follow the last relay down.
Drop to the court floor early.
Fall in behind the plunder porters.

nodes.body: You study Korvane himself, cold and careful, noting robes far too dry for this temple. The lead folio chained to his belt, and soft shoes that have walked through worse than water. At his throat, on silver wire, hangs a coral sigil, twin to the singers. The man is armored in preparations, layered in them. But his eyes go always back to the font, again and again, like a debtor to a purse he does not own yet.

choices.label: Slip down as the court breaks up.
Count his pacing like a jailers rounds.

nodes.body: You slip down the service stair as the court breaks up beneath you, captains barking march orders in the flat voice the Coil saves for hirelings. The temple is pouring itself toward the deep like sand through a glass, every passage flowing one direction. The deep approaches lie ahead of the crowd, but only just, and only if you move now and keep moving.

choices.label: Make for the deep approaches.

nodes.body: Gorses face proves worth the wait. Fear sits on it like a badly fitted helm, and the captain keeps glancing at the water stair as if his grandmothers wisdom lived down it. Once, strangely, he looks straight at the loft lattice, as if he felt eyes there and wished them well on their way. You file that look with the other valuables and slip down toward the deep approaches, spending strength you are starting to miss.

choices.label: Make for the deep approaches.

nodes.body: You follow the last relay at lamplighter distance through the emptying halls, close enough to smell torch pitch, far enough to be nothing. The torches shrink to sparks down the processional stair ahead. Then a side arch opens on your right, breathing older air. Offering the quieter deep approaches to anyone brave enough to leave the light and go down the dark way alone.

choices.label: Take the side arch into the old dark.

nodes.body: One captain below picks your lattice gap to stare at, for no reason but a soldiers luck, and eyes meet through the carved screen. The moment stretches, snaps. Boots drum up the loft stair from both ends at once, and the war court acquires an audience it intends to keep. You watch the rest of the planning from much closer, under guard, and the plan works perfectly for everyone who is not you.

You stay for one sentence too many, and the court rises all at once for the march. Every stair and arch fills with ordered ranks in the time it takes to reach the loft door, and the loft becomes a trap with an excellent view. The sweep finds you pinned there before the bells even change tempo, and the rite gains four witnesses in chains.

The deep approaches run under the oldest stone in the temple, and every way down is spoken for. Left, the processional stair rings with marching torches. Right, a dry spillway chute yawns black and silent. Ahead, the bell shaft drops past dressed stone with a rope hanging ready. And past all three, faint and patient and closer than it has ever been, sounds the one low note that never ends. The seal is already half singing, and the night is spending fast.

choices.label: Ride the spillway chute down.
Trust the chute and skip the brake.
Go down the bell shaft rope.
Shadow the relays down the stair.
Send Sable ahead to map the floor.

nodes.body: The spillway chute is a childs nightmare of a slide, shell grit and speed and no second thoughts permitted. You learn the heel brake in the first heartbeat and apply it for the rest of the drop. The bottom arrives as a shell heap in a crypt antechamber, soft as landings go, filthy as they come, and the bells are suddenly very near, felt in the floor as much as heard.

choices.label: Brake hard and land in the antechamber.
Surf the shell heap to save your boots.

nodes.body: The bell shaft rope runs down past dressed stone older than the temple above it, courses laid by hands that measured in forgotten spans. Your boots touch a floor of flagstones fitted tight as bookbinding. A passage leads off, breathing cold, straight toward the note, and the shafts circle of torchlight shrinks above you like a closing eye as you leave it behind.

choices.label: Follow the cold passage to the note.
Swing wide to skip the last courses.

nodes.body: You take the processional stair in the relays own shadow, close enough to smell the torch pitch, matching their step for sixty pounding heartbeats. At the last landing you peel off into a side vault as the column wheels toward the rite floor, torch glow flooding past the vault mouth like a river past a snag. The drowned door lies somewhere beyond that glow, and the singing is louder here.
