choices.label: Peel into the side vault at the landing.
Hold in the column one flight more.

nodes.body: The chute forks mid slide where no map said fork, and momentum votes before you can. The wrong throat narrows, and narrows, and holds. The temple keeps what it catches, the way it has always kept things, with the bells for company and the shell dust settling slow in the dark around the last of your season.

The column halts mid stair on some captains whim, and the torches wheel about like startled birds. The relays shadow shrinks to exactly nothing around four strangers standing on the processional steps with no torches of their own. Taken in ranks, marched in ranks, you end the night in a pen with a view of the rite, which is the cruelest seat in the house.

The pens approach runs through the old buttery, ration jars racked in rows like a misers pantry, a tide clock dripping its count in a wall niche. The cough is close now, and so is everything that guards it. Four ways through offer themselves, each with its own risk and its own cover, and the tide clock keeps dripping either way, spending the night one drop at a time.

choices.label: Thread the buttery jar racks.
Crawl the drain gutter under the floor.
Push a loaded shell barrow through.
Detour by the drained side aisle.
Check the brine vats for another way.
Read the tide clock before choosing.

nodes.body: You thread the buttery jar rack by jar rack, close enough to read the Coil tally marks on every lid. Marsh mead, salt eel, ships bread, an army provisioned in a temple. The racks stand close and the shadows stand closer, and somewhere past the last row a work whistle carries from the pens gallery. Ordinary as any dockyard, wrong as everything else here.

choices.label: Slip through dry mouthed and empty handed.
Pocket two jars for the prisoners.
Sample the Coil mead, one swallow.

nodes.body: The drain gutter under the buttery floor takes you at a crawl, tight and wet and blind, floor slabs a hands width overhead. Boots cross the slabs twice while you hold breath and count. There is stand up room only at the far end, Reed promised, and the promise gets longer with every soaked elbow, and then it gets kept.

choices.label: Crawl on to the stand up end.
Stand up early where the slab looks loose.

nodes.body: You borrow a shell barrow from the buttery corner, load it with jars, and push it through the working passage at a porters pace, heads down. A barrow is a passport in any working place, Sable says, and she is nearly right. Gray hoods step around you. Nobody looks at faces above a loaded barrow. The wheel, though, has opinions, and squeaks them once.

choices.label: Park the barrow and slip the last stretch.
Push the barrow through the far arch.

nodes.body: The long way around runs by the drained side aisle, old flood marks shoulder high on the pillars, statues of drowned saints watching the detour with patient stone faces. It is slow and empty and quiet enough to hear your own pulse. The saints have watched worse pass under them, their faces say, and mostly it drowned anyway.

choices.label: Rejoin at the pens gallery.
Cut behind the brine vats instead.

nodes.body: The buttery ends at the pens passage, marked by the tide clocks drip and one thin cough through the wall. From the gallery beyond drifts a whistled work signal, two notes, bored, regular, a man telling the dark he is still awake. The last rack of jars stands between you and the answer to everything.

choices.label: Answer the work signal and walk in.
Wait for the signal to pass, then move.

nodes.body: The gutter crawl ends soaked to the shoulders in a dark alcove. And you wring out what wrings while the cold banks itself in your bones for later withdrawal. The pens gallery opens ahead, rush lit and quiet, and the crawl already feels like a fair price for arriving underneath everyones expectations.

choices.label: Move to the pens gallery.

nodes.body: The barrow parks at the passage mouth in honest shadow, one more piece of dockyard furniture. And the working passage gives onto the pens gallery unwatched. You leave the jars stacked like a promise to nobody and step through with empty hands and a working heart.

choices.label: Slip into the pens gallery.

nodes.body: The side aisle rejoins at the pens gallery, the detour paid in time and repaid in silence. Behind, the drowned saints keep their watch over the empty aisle. Ahead, rush light and iron and the small stubborn cough, closer now than it has been since Barrowmere.

choices.label: Enter the pens gallery.

nodes.body: The last stretch runs behind a rank of brine vats, salt fog hanging off their open tops, eyes stinging, boots silent on wet stone. The vat rank ends at the pens gallery corner, and the gallery opens beyond it. Rush lit, iron barred, guarded by one chair and one sleeping man.

choices.label: Come out at the pens gallery.

nodes.body: One ration jar gets sampled on a dry tongue, and Coil marsh mead turns out to be stronger than any small beer ever brewed. The buttery spins gently. The jar rack catches an elbow, the elbow catches a shelf, and gray hoods collect their unsteady guests from the wreckage with something close to sympathy, marching them to the pens they came to empty.

The barrow wheel finds its voice at the worst arch, a squeal like a gutted hinge. And the working passage fills both ends with hands that know their own barrows and their own wheels. The jars are counted, the faces above them are counted twice, and the arithmetic comes out wrong for exactly four people.

The diver pens hold the drained pool in a horseshoe, nine barred cells around water gone to black glass. A warder sleeps under the key board by the tide clock, one boot up, snoring in a minor key. And at the second grate a face waits, gray and steady, exactly as Reed described her. Old Marta the peat reeve, eyes finding yours, lips shaping one word with no sound at all. Floodtide.

choices.label: Lift the keys above the warders hat.
Let Sable pick the second grate.
Take Martas whisper through the bars first.
Wait out the relief and lose the hour.
Have Sable spring the hound pen for noise.
Lift the floor grate and map from below.

nodes.body: The keys hang a hands width above the warders hat, and you lift them one at a time so none swing and kiss their neighbors. The chair creaks once, like a question asked in another room. You wait it out with a key between two fingers and your pulse in your ears. The snore resumes its rhythm, and the ring comes away whole into your palm, heavy as the answer.

choices.label: Open the grates one by one.
Open the far cells first, quietly.
Hurry the ring off its hook.

nodes.body: Sables picks talk to the second grates lock in little clicks, a patient conversation with an old and honest mechanism that never learned to expect cleverness. The wards give up their argument one by one. The bolt draws back sweet as a spoon from pudding, and Martas hand is already at the gap, steadying the door against its own eagerness.

choices.label: Open the grates on greased hinges.

nodes.body: Martas whisper comes through the grate first, dry and precise as a freight bond. Eleven alive, counting Cols mother and the child Whin. The warder sleeps, but the relief comes when the tide clock fills, so count your minutes. And one thing more, pushed through the iron like a knife handle first. The Coil has promised its diggers the divers as payment when the door opens, and people as wages, and Marta lets that sit.

choices.label: Free them all, near cells first.
Hear Martas full account first.
Take her muster name by name.

nodes.body: The grates open one by one on hinges fed with lamp grease, and the fen folk rise stiff and silent, weeks of straw and patience unfolding into people. A mother lifts the coughing child, and the childs arms go around her neck without waking. Eleven faces gather in the gallerys one rush light, and all of them ask the same question with no sound at all. What now, and it is a very good question.

choices.label: Whisper the plan in the empty cell.

nodes.body: You open the far cells first and let the fen folk wake their own neighbors, hands over mouths. The practiced hush of people who have rehearsed this in whispers for weeks. The pens fill with standing shadows, stretching, flexing, becoming a crew again. The second grate comes last, and Whin is lifted still half asleep, the cough smothered gently into a borrowed cloak that swallows her whole.

choices.label: Gather them for the plan.
Light the pen lamps to work faster.

nodes.body: Martas answer comes flat with fury. The Coil dives the captives at every ebb tide, down into the flooded deep to clear silt from the seal stones and thread the raising chains. And the water is colder than sense every time. Whin dives tomorrow unless tonight goes right, she says, and lets the arithmetic of that do its own shouting.

choices.label: Set the plan before the tide clock fills.

nodes.body: The relief comes early, the tide clock not yet full, and finds iron grates standing open on an empty pen. There is nowhere to put eleven people and a cough on ten heartbeats notice. The alarm bell rings the wing awake, and by the end of it the pens hold more tenants than they started with, and the tide clock fills on schedule, for whatever that is worth.

One key swings into its neighbor with a chime small as a teaspoon on china, and the warders hand closes on your wrist before his eyes even open. Old reflexes in an old chair. His shout brings the wing down on the pens, boots and iron and rush light, and the last thing the freeing accomplishes is teaching the temple to hang its keys farther from the chairs.

The plan whispers itself together in an empty cell. Eleven people cannot sneak past a live terrace, but at floodtide the temple empties to the rite, every pole drawn down to the deep. So they hide until then, armed with the warders knives, and go out the terrace sluice on your signal. Marta grips your arm hard at the end of it, and find what the Coil fears, she says, and break it. Behind her the fen folk are already prying pins in the drain grate.

choices.label: Hide them in the drained font vault.
Hide them in the choir loft above.
Move them to the sluice grate now.
Let them rush the gate armed as they are.
Split the party, two and two.
Swear the pen to Martas freight oath.

nodes.body: The drained font vault off the pens swallows eleven people and their breathing one rung at a time. Water jars and the warders own bread go down after them, and Whin falls asleep at last against her mothers shoulder on the way down, mid protest. You ease the lid stone back over the dark with a straws width of air left at the rim, and the pens above stand empty and innocent.

choices.label: Set the signal and go to the gallery.
Leave Sable with the hidden.
Seat the lid true and airless.

nodes.body: The choir loft over the pens opens off the bell rope stair, deep in the dust of a hundred quiet years, and the bell tackle clatter below covers eleven sets of climbing footsteps completely. The fen folk settle among broken pew wrack like patient ghosts, armed with kitchen knives and peat law, and Marta posts herself at the rope head where she can watch the stair. Whin sleeps. The dust resettles. The loft keeps them.

choices.label: Set the signal and take the stairs.

nodes.body: You move them now, the whole pen in file along the west wall at a crawl, every buckle muffled in rag, every breath budgeted. The terrace sluice waits at the wall foot with its pins already loosened, fen work, quick and neat. Eleven people fold into the drain mouth dark one at a time, water at their ankles and their hearts in their teeth. And the grate settles back over them like a lid over live coals.

choices.label: Climb back and make for the gallery.
Let the child rest at the wall turn.

nodes.body: The signal is set and said back twice. One long heron call down the sluice at floodtide, repeated cell to hiding place to drain, a thread of breath holding eleven lives strung along it. There is nothing more to do for them now except the thing you actually came to do. The court gallery stairs wait ahead, and the bells below have begun, very slowly, to gather pace.

choices.label: Take the court gallery stairs.

nodes.body: Sable stays with the hidden, shrugging like it is nothing, fooling nobody. She lays her three visible knives out in front of her like cutlery at a formal dinner and settles against the vault lid to wait. The way to the gallery is lighter without her and lonelier by exactly the same amount. And you feel her absence at your right shoulder like a draft the rest of the wing.

choices.label: Go on to the gallery without her.

nodes.body: The climb back from the sluice runs through the boneyard drain, cold to the knees and dark as the inside of a pocket. Above you the temple has begun to stir, torch relays flowing down toward the deep in ordered pairs. Floodtide gathering the whole Coil like a drawn breath. You come up into the mid level with the signal set behind you and the gallery stairs ahead, and no time left to spend twice.

choices.label: Come up and make for the gallery.
Borrow a sconce torch against the dark.

nodes.body: The cough comes at the worst turn of the west wall, one small chest heaving in the shadow line while a torch swings toward the sound. Eleven people press flat as paint and it is not enough. The shout goes up, and the whole rescue is marched back to the pens at pole point, you among them, and Whin cries the entire way, which is somehow the worst part of all of it.

The fen folk refuse the wait, and you cannot entirely blame them. Knives up, they make an honest, doomed rush at the pen gate, and the gate answers with numbers, the way gates always answer. The gallery becomes a churn of poles and shouting. The party is swept back out the sluice alone, and the rescue breaks on its own courage, which is the cruelest way anything breaks.

The great refectory hall booms between tides, Coil hands feeding in shifts at long tables under torch smoke. By the fire pit dozes a pale giant of a champion with a boat hook across his knees, big enough to make Bram look reasonable. Three ways across offer themselves. The gallery screen behind the feast, carved and shadowed. The roof trusses above the smoke. And the forge annex, loud with hammer work and nobody watching its far door.

choices.label: Walk the gallery screen behind the feast.
Cross the roof trusses above the smoke.
Thread the forge annex by hammer noise.
Check the stacked boxes by the door first.
Listen to the officers table a while.

nodes.body: The gallery screen walks you behind the feast, carved saints for cover at every stride, table talk washing past about wages and floodtide and whether drowned silver spends. The champions doze sits between you and the far arch, boat hook rising and falling on his chest like a moored boat. He sleeps the way mountains sleep, present, and subject to waking.

choices.label: Shuffle the screen past the champion.
Rush the gap between feeding shifts.
Lift the champions boat hook for a trophy.
Freeze when the hall hound circles near.

nodes.body: The roof trusses cross above the smoke, a beam walk over the whole feeding hall, torchlight below running like a river under a bridge. The far wall plate is a long cold crossing away, and the smoke that hides you also hides the beam, one plank of shadow at a time. Arms out, eyes forward, and the hall roars on below, ignorant as weather.

choices.label: Cross plate to plate without stopping.
Rest at the kingpost and read the hall.
Jump the gap where two trusses part.

nodes.body: The forge annex route runs on heat and hammer noise, bell chain lengths cooling on racks, quench steam drifting in sheets. One smith works late with his back to the door, beating a link true, three blows and a breath, three blows and a breath. The far door stands past him, warm and unguarded, and the rhythm is a road if you can walk it.

choices.label: Time each step to the hammer blows.
Learn his rhythm first, then cross.
Slip past while he quenches, blind in steam.

nodes.body: The screen finishes at a shuffle, the champions snore never breaking, and the far arch takes you in with the feast none the wiser. Behind, the hall goes on feeding, and somewhere in it a bench of Reedcloaks argues wages, which is worth remembering. Hired men argue wages. Believers do not.

choices.label: Go through to the plunder vault.

nodes.body: You rush the screen in the gap between shifts, quick and costly, breath spent like coin, and the far arch arrives before the next bench fills. Behind you a serving hand frowns at a shadow that is no longer there, decides the smoke is lying, and goes back to the pot. Speed buys silence sometimes. Tonight it took the deal.

choices.label: Go through to the plunder vault.

nodes.body: The trusses cross plate to plate with the smoke for a blanket, arms burning by the midpoint, the hall below feeding on in its own noise. A dropped spoon rings on stone somewhere and fifty heads turn toward it and none of them up. The far wall plate arrives under your hands like a finish line, and the vault side drop waits below.

choices.label: Drop to the vault side and go in.

nodes.body: You break the crossing at the kingpost for a rest, and the whole Coil order lays itself out below like a diagram. Shift bells, table ranks, the officers doorway, the champions fire. Knowledge pours up through the smoke for the price of aching arms, and when you move again, you move knowing where everything in the hall will be when it matters.

choices.label: Finish the crossing to the vault.

nodes.body: The annex threads rack by rack while the smith hammers, each step timed to a blow, tongs and chain and quench steam between you and the far door. The heat sits on both cheeks like hands while the count runs, three blows and a breath. You move on the blows and stand on the breaths, and the racks pass one forge length at a time.

choices.label: Come out at the tackle store.
Borrow the long tongs on the way.

nodes.body: You learn his rhythm first, three blows and a breath, then a long pause every ninth for the quench. The crossing happens inside his own noise, invisible as a beat inside music. And the far door takes you through warm and unremarked while the ninth link hisses steam behind you.

choices.label: Come out at the tackle store.

nodes.body: The champions doze ends on instinct, one pale hand closing the hall doors with a boom that stops fifty conversations at once. The feast rises from its benches around four strangers with nowhere left to be strange, and the boat hook, it turns out, is not ceremonial. The pens gain four tenants, and the hall gets a story with its supper.

One truss plate sheds its edge under a boot, and the long fall goes through torch smoke into the middle of supper. The hall pauses one beat at the sound, the way halls do, and then the benches empty toward it. What the fall spares, the landing collects, and the rite gains a witness who arrived by air.

The plunder vault behind the hall holds the taken families goods, stacked in lots with tally marks, nets and christening spoons and a childs toy boat, all of it priced for someone. It is a pawn shop of other peoples lives, and it is the angriest room in the temple, although nothing in it moves. Somewhere in the lots are Reeds familys things. Nobody looks for them. Everybody thinks about it.

choices.label: Search the vault fast and leave it straight.
Read the lead tablets in the last lot.
Try the barred inner vault door.

nodes.body: The tackle store past the annex racks the whole rig of the raising, bell chains coiled to the roof, dive weights, hooks, and lamp oil in jars enough to light a town. Every chain length is sized and tagged for the seal font, the raising planned to the link. It is the Coils toolbox, and you are standing in it with time to be creative.

choices.label: Read the chain rig and pull its pin.
Weigh the lamp oil as a weapon.
Carry off an armful of dive gear.
Sabotage the forge bellows quietly.

nodes.body: The vault searches fast and clean, and a route board on the wall gives up the nights geography, the rite floor, the choir stair, the diver assignments. Elowen copies it in chalk shorthand and leaves the board hanging straight. The vault door closes behind you on all that priced grief, and the service stair climbs toward the officers voices.

choices.label: Take the service stair toward the court.

nodes.body: The last lot holds lead tablets, old temple script under Coil chalk marks, the drowned wardens own liturgy defaced with sums and stage directions. Elowen reads three lines and goes pale in the rush light. They are not translating it, she whispers. They are turning it backward. There is something worth reading here, if there were time, and there is never time.

choices.label: Leave the tablets and take the stair.
Stay reading one tablet too long.
Copy the cipher marks for Elowen.

nodes.body: The chain rig reads like a confession. Every length sized to the font, every pulley greased, the raising planned to the link and the minute. And one master pulley holds the whole system, its pin worn bright and loose in its block. You pocket the pin on the way past, and somewhere above, the raising becomes a much more interesting problem than its planners know.

choices.label: Pocket the pulley pin and take the stair.
Slick the store floor with oil first.

nodes.body: The lamp oil weighs itself as a weapon in your hands, jars enough to light the store, the annex, and every plan the Coil has racked in between. Cold arithmetic, what burns and what alarms, what a fire buys and what it costs the hidden eleven if the temple wakes early. The jars wait, heavy with both answers.

choices.label: Leave the oil and take the stair.
Tip one jar for insurance.

nodes.body: The oil jar tips for insurance and catches early, and the store goes up like a festival. The raising is delayed one tide while the Coil hunts its arsonists with everything it owns, which would be a victory if the company were not caught in the third sweep, smoked out of a drain with the evidence still on its sleeves. The rite waits. The pens do not.

The way on from the vault is a service stair rising toward the court gallery, officers voices somewhere above trading tide times and march orders. The bell note swells a half step as you climb, as if the temple were tuning itself for the night ahead. And the stairs deliver you toward the one room where the whole plan will be said out loud.

choices.label: Climb toward the officers voices.

nodes.body: The way on from the tackle store climbs the same service stair, the master pulley pin riding in your pocket like a stolen tooth. Above, officers voices trade tide times. Below, the raising rig waits to discover its missing piece at the worst possible moment, which is exactly when you intend it to.

choices.label: Climb toward the officers voices.

nodes.body: The smith turns for his quench bucket a blow early, and eyes meet eyes across the chain racks. He is not a fighter, but the forge bell is right there, and he hammers it with a professionals economy before anyone can cross the floor. The annex fills, the wing follows, and the night ends in irons that some cousin of this very forge probably made.

One armful of the families goods becomes two, and the second stack tips with a clatter of christening spoons that rings like an accusation. The vault door fills with tally keepers who count strangers fastest of all. The goods go back on the shelves, priced. The company goes to the pens, also priced, and the lesson costs more than the spoons were worth.

The grotto passage breathes incense, sweet and gray, and somewhere ahead a tide organ moans on the swell like a sleeping choir. Shell wards hang across the dark on gut lines, spirals of coral that chatter at a touch, and through all of it runs the thin thread of song, one human voice keeping the seals note company. The passage offers a dormitory row, a tackle gallery above, and the flooded margin below. All three go where the song goes.

choices.label: Pass the novice cells on the slate count.
Cross the bell tackle gallery above.
Wade the grottos flooded margin.
Read the incense smoke drift first.
Study the shell wards before touching one.

nodes.body: The novice dormitory runs cell by curtained cell, pale robed sleepers breathing in rows, one lamp turned low at the far end. By the arch hangs a headcount slate, eleven marks, neat as a manifest. Sable reads it and holds up ten fingers, then one, then points at the cells and shrugs. The slate says eleven. The count is somebody elses promise, and you are about to walk through the middle of it.

choices.label: Slip past on the posted count.
Leave Elowens hush mark on the arch.
Recount the sleepers yourself.

nodes.body: The bell tackle gallery hangs above the grotto, ropes rising into dark. Each labeled with a note mark in chalk, do not touch written in every language rope speaks. The handholds run where the ropes are not, iron rings and beam ends, a crossing for careful hands. Below, the grotto glows faint with incense light, and the song threads up between the ropes like smoke.

choices.label: Cross quick between the labeled ropes.
Cross patient, testing every hold.
Steady yourself on a labeled rope.

nodes.body: The flooded margin wades rim to rim around the grotto, incense smoke lying flat on the water like a second skin. Bone flutes stand racked at the tide line, instruments of the order the Coil stole this temple from, and the song circles closer with every careful stride. Cold to the knees, quiet as fog, and the den glow rising ahead through the haze.

choices.label: Wade it cold, boots carried.
Walk the fallen organ pipe dry.
Try a racked bone flute against the song.
Watch the novice at her tide chart first.

nodes.body: The dormitory end arrives past the last curtained cell. The slate count left exactly as posted, eleven sleepers verified by eleven held breaths. Beyond, the singers own passage opens, hung with more shell wards, and the song is close enough now to have words in it. Old ones, patient ones, in no language the fen wives ever taught anybody.

choices.label: Take the singers passage.

nodes.body: Elowen leaves a chalk hush mark on the last cell arch, a scholars trick against singers, she says, drawn from the tablet lore and improved by nerve. Whatever it does, the dormitory sleeps deeper for it, breathing slowing behind you like a tide going out. The singers passage takes you with the mark working at your back.

choices.label: Go on with the hush mark working.
Go back and thicken the chalk line.

nodes.body: The tackle gallery crosses hold to hold between the labeled ropes, arms trembling by the last span. The final stretch runs out of iron rings entirely and finishes in a slide down bare stone to the singers passage, undignified and silent, which is the correct order of priorities. The song never wavers. It has heard nothing, or it does not care.

choices.label: Slide down to the singers passage.

nodes.body: The gallery crosses the patient way, every hold tested twice. And at the far end a coil of spare bell line hangs on a peg, good hemp, sound as the day it was laid up. Good rope is good rope, Bram whispers, borrowing it, and the drop to the singers passage goes easy with the line paying out smooth as a promise.

choices.label: Drop to the passage, rope and all.

nodes.body: The flooded margin finishes cold to the waist, boots carried high, and the far rim takes you up shivering with incense in your teeth. The wading was slow and honest and the cold banks itself somewhere it can be collected later. Ahead the den glow warms the haze, and the song stands so near now it has a breath in it.

choices.label: Pad on to the singers den.

nodes.body: A fallen pipe of the tide organ makes a brass road over the black water, dry passage rim to rim, and it rings faintly under each step no matter the care, one soft note per stride. The song ahead never falters, but once, just once, it leaves a space exactly the size of your last footfall, and then flows on. Noted, the space says. Continue.

choices.label: Step off the pipe at the den mouth.

nodes.body: The slate count was somebody elses promise, and it was wrong. One novice lies awake in her cell dark, counting back, and her count comes up four strangers long. The dormitory wakes in a single indrawn breath around you, pale robes rising from every curtain at once. And the grotto files its guests where the Coil files everything, behind iron, counted twice.

One labeled rope offers itself as a handhold, and the note mark keeps its promise. Somewhere above, a bell the size of a cart answers its rope with a peal of brass that rolls through the grotto like a tide of sound, and every pale robe in the wing arrives at a run to see who rang for service. The gallery holds its catch until they climb up for it.

The singers den opens like the inside of a shell, and Serah the Whisper sits at her tide glass bowl with her back to the door, singing to the water. The coral key hangs on a ward hook among chattering shells, an arms length from her unguarded neck. And without turning, without breaking the song by more than a breath, she speaks to your reflection in the bowl. Come in, wardens, she says. The door remembers you even if the Coil does not.

choices.label: Lift the coral key from the ward hook.
Step out and parley with the Whisper.
Rush the hook and take the key by speed.
Back out and go keyless to the court.
Touch the chattering wards to still them.

nodes.body: You lift the key from the ward hook a shell at a time while the Whisper sings to her bowl, two fingers stilling the chatter, patience doing the rest. The coral comes warm off the hook, warm as a held hand, humming faintly with the song it was cut to serve. The singer never turns, and her song never pauses. Some thefts are permitted, the silence seems to say, and some are only postponed.

choices.label: Wrap the key and slip out.
Glance into her tide glass bowl.

nodes.body: You step out and parley instead, and the Whisper names the company one by one to her bowl without turning, faces read from the water. She is bored of the Coil and its debts, she says, bored of masters who cannot sing. The key can be had for a price she never quite names, circling it the way her song circles the seals note, close, closer, never touching. Bargaining with a singer is singing, and she knows the tune better.

choices.label: Pay her price and take the key.
Decline the price and part friendly.
Ask the bowl your own question.
Trade her the temples gossip instead.

nodes.body: You rush the den floor fast and flat, and the bowl water leaps as the ward hooks shells scream brass. The key comes off in your hand mid stride, warm and humming, and the Whispers song breaks off into something that is not quite a laugh. Run, then, wardens, she says to her bowl, mild as morning, and the door will still ask who taught you manners.

choices.label: Run for the court stairs with it.
Turn at the door for one look back.

nodes.body: You back out of the den unrobbed, empty handed and unhunted, and the Whisper sings on to her bowl as if the visit were weather. The long way around to the court gallery costs a quarter hour and buys no new enemies, which Sable calls a fair fen trade. The choir door will have to open some other way, or not at all.

choices.label: Take the long way to the court.

nodes.body: The key wraps in a rag against its own soft chatter, and the den falls behind, still singing, still unturned. The court gallery stairs climb ahead through the incense dark, and the coral warms your pocket like a coal that has opinions. The Whisper let you take it. Everyone walking away knows that, and nobody says it out loud.

choices.label: Climb to the choir loft lattice.

nodes.body: The price settles as a rumor to be delivered someday, to an address she sings rather than says, and the key comes over her shoulder warm from her own neck. The Whispers last words follow you out through the wards. The door remembers its wardens, she sings, and the shells take up the phrase and chatter it after you like children repeating a lesson.

choices.label: Climb to the loft with the key warm.

nodes.body: The parley ends empty handed but friendly, which with singers may be the better purse. The Whisper turns back to her bowl, and her song shifts as you go, covering your footsteps to the court stairs the way fog covers a boat. A gift, or a receipt. With singers, Elowen whispers, it is always both.

choices.label: Climb to the choir loft lattice.

nodes.body: The key wins at a sprint and a snatch, and the den rings behind you like a struck cupboard, shells screaming down every passage at once. The court stairs take you two at a time ahead of the answering feet, lungs burning, the coral hot in your fist. Loud, Sable pants, but paid for, and the stairs agree to disagree.

choices.label: Take the loft stairs two at a time.
Pause to listen for pursuit first.

nodes.body: The long way around to the court gallery runs keyless but quiet, the song fading behind like a tide withdrawing, and the officers voices growing ahead. No coral, no choir door, and no new enemies either. The night will have to be won on the floor instead of behind it, and the company walks taller for knowing which.

choices.label: Climb to the choir loft lattice.

nodes.body: The bowl sees what the singer does not, your reach reflected at the wrong moment in the tide glass. The song flattens to one note, and the wards answer it, every shell in the grotto closing its chatter into a single pointing chord. Footsteps arrive from three passages at once, and the Whisper finally turns, unhurried, to watch her room collect you.

The ward hook was trapped for the unsung, and the bone dart is no longer than a finger. The sting is small, the softness that follows it is not. The den goes warm and dark around the edges while the song, at last, stops, and the last thing the grotto offers is the Whispers voice, mildly disappointed, saying manners, wardens, manners.

The shell wards chatter at the first wrong step, a gossip of brass running passage to passage faster than boots can. And the grotto wing shuts itself around the noise like a fist. The company backs out the way it came, wards screaming the route ahead of every stride, and the visit spends itself with nothing bought and the whole wing awake behind it.

The rite floor opens under the whole weight of the temple, a flooded rotunda ringed with pillars, and at its heart the second seal font hangs dripping on its chain cradle before a great drowned door. Ringers rank a side gallery, hand bells hammering the rite time. Reedcloaks pool in from the processional stair, and Korvane unchains his lead folio with the care of a man opening a wound. Under it all the bell note swells toward floodtide like water behind a dam. The night narrows to one choice of doors.

choices.label: Open the drowned choir way with the coral key.
Go down the colonnade stair through the shadows.
Sound the marsh calls and turn the Reedcloaks.
Climb through the ringers gallery above the floor.

nodes.body: The coral key warms in your fist beside a blank wall panel, and finds its slot the way a key finds a lock in the dark. Stone swallows coral with a sound like a sigh. A drowned wardens choir way opens before you, narrow and black and unbreathed for centuries, running behind the rotunda wall toward the door itself. The air tastes of salt and old duty, and the bells come through the stone like a heartbeat that is not yours.

choices.label: Follow the choir way at a hunters trot.
Read the warden carvings as you go.
Widen a sound slot for a proper look first.
Search the wardens vestry niche as you pass.
Study the choir murals where the slots pause.

nodes.body: The choir way runs striped with sound slots, and the rite assembles itself in snatches as you pass. Ringers, ranks, the font on its cradle, Korvanes open folio, each slice of torchlight a page of the same bad story. Then the passage ends at a postern set behind the drowned door itself, close enough to touch the cradle chains shadow. The wardens built themselves a back way to the one door that matters.

choices.label: Take the postern to the doors blind side.
Stop at the hinge slot and learn the singers breath.

nodes.body: The carvings run the choir way like a wardens catechism, cut in the old strokes. The king below did not drown, he was stopped, and the seal is not a lock, it is a lullaby. And the last stroke, cut deepest of all, reads like a hand on your shoulder, and wake him for nothing, it says, for no crown is worth the flood he brings. You stand in the dark with the truth of the temple, and the bells keep time beyond the wall.

choices.label: Carry that truth to the doors blind side.
Hunt the carvings for the seals own weakness first.
Pry at the deepest carving for the crown behind it.
Study the last mural panel a breath longer.

nodes.body: The doors blind side is stone cold as a dead hearth, and the postern crack gives you the font and its seal stone a spear length away. On the far side of everything you came to stop, Korvanes voice begins the waking words, patient and dry, like water explaining itself to a shore. The seal note climbs a step. Whatever you are going to do, the night wants it done now.

choices.label: Move on the rite.
Widen the postern crack for a better view first.

nodes.body: The weakness is written plain, in a wardens honest hand. The seal sings itself, it needs no master, but a wrong note breaks the verse. Strike the font while it sings, and the song starts over from silence, every verse of it, from the very first breath. You carry the knowledge to the doors blind side like a knife carried point down, careful and ready, while the waking words begin beyond the stone.

choices.label: Move on the rite with the knife of it.

nodes.body: You widen one sound slot with impatient fingers, and mortar dust sifts out into torchlight on the rotunda side. A pale face turns up at exactly the wrong moment. The choir way becomes a stone sleeve with both ends held, and you are taken out of it like a ferret from a burrow, blinking, furious, and caught.

The sunken colonnade stair hugs the rotunda wall, and pillar shadows stripe every landing like the bars of a patient cage. Below, the Reedcloak ranks face the door with their backs to you, rank on rank of poles. And far down at the bottom the font cradle glows in its lamp ring, the only lit stage in a dark theater, with the seal stone as the single prop. Every way down is a wager, and the bells are counting the stakes.

choices.label: Descend shadow to shadow between bell beats.
Take the cornice ledge behind the pillar tops.
Join the last rank of the procession itself.
Take the pillar forest floor on your belly.

nodes.body: The bell beats make a ladder for footfalls, and down and hold, and down and hold. You descend inside the rhythm, close enough to the ranks to count rope scars on the nearest necks. Then a captain turns to scan the stair, one heartbeat after your shadow folds into a pillars. His eyes walk the steps you just left, slow as a paid inspector, while the bells hold the whole night steady.

choices.label: Hold frozen through his sweep.
Flow on during the next beat anyway.
Glide down two flights in one beat to be past him.
Count the lamp ring before the last flight.

nodes.body: The cornice ledge behind the pillar tops is a masons walk, never meant for boots at speed, a hands width of dressed stone above the whole assembly. The rite lies below like a map of itself, ranks and font and door. The ledge ends at a drowned kings statue taller than a house, whose ear is a step and whose shoulder is a drop, straight down into the doors blind shadow.

choices.label: Climb down the drowned kings statue.
Run the cornice upright to save time.

nodes.body: The sweep passes over your stillness like light over stone, finding nothing because you have become nothing. The captain turns back to the door, satisfied, bored. You take the last flight inside the next three bell beats and reach the rotunda floor where the fonts lamp ring begins, close enough now to hear the seal stones low note under the peals.

choices.label: Move on the rite.

nodes.body: You flow on during the next beat anyway, and it costs a scrape of boot on grit. One ear below twitches. You shove the breath back down your own throat and hold, one shadow among shadows, until the ear forgets. The rotunda floor arrives at last with your heart trying to leave through your teeth, and the lamp ring glowing a pole cast ahead.

choices.label: Move on the rite.

nodes.body: The drowned kings statue bears you down feature by feature. Ear, jaw, shoulder, each hold older than any kingdom you can name. The drop lands soft on old silt in the doors blind shadow, and the waking words begin somewhere above your head, dry and patient, spoken to stone that has waited long enough to be patient too.

choices.label: Move on the rite.
Take the statues front in the open instead.

nodes.body: One rank wheels on some order you never heard, and torchlight finds you mid stair with nowhere left to fold. The colonnade becomes a short parade. You are walked down every remaining step in a cage of poles and presented at the font like a gift, and Korvane looks up from his folio with the mild interest of a man handed extra ink.

The cornice sheds its edge under one boot, and the pillar top tilts away into torch glow. The rotunda floor rises through bell time, peal and peal and peal. The rite pauses one beat for the sound, no more, and goes on without you, and the bells close over the place where you fell.

The marsh calls sit cold on your lips in a side vault off the rotunda. Reeds lessons, learned beside a wrecked barge a lifetime ago, now aimed like a weapon. Below, Gorse stands apart from the Coil master with his honor guard around him, a hired captain watching his own band being spent on another mans flood, and not enjoying the arithmetic. The Reedcloaks obey their calls the way rivers obey their banks. One right note could move an army. One wrong one could end you.

choices.label: Sound the hunt loosed call, pointed at the master.
Sound the all clear, then walk to Gorse openly.
Sound the calls at half breath to stay quiet.
Approach the honor guard captain under a plain flag.

nodes.body: The long falling call rolls through the rotunda like cold water, and every Reedcloak head lifts at once. Hunt loosed. Hunted where. Ranks ripple, poles swing to face four directions, and eyes find Korvane at his font one suspicious heartbeat before the captains start shouting orders that contradict each other. The rites clean machine grinds on sand you poured into it, and the night belongs, briefly, to whoever keeps their head.

choices.label: Slip down through the confusion toward the font.
Sound it again from a new vault to feed the panic.
Sound the hunt call a third time for good measure.
Harvest the confusion for gear and watchwords.

nodes.body: The all clear buys you a corridor of doubt, and you walk it openly, palms shown. Gorses honor guard parts on his flat gesture. The captain looks you over like a boat he might buy, unhurried, missing nothing. Then he speaks in trade tongue, low and even. His grandmother forbade this water to her whole line. So what, he asks, do drowned wardens want with a living captain, and the question is a door standing open.

choices.label: Tell him the truth, the Coil will feed his band to the door.
Offer him the vault plunder to march home now.
Demand he stand down in the marshals name.
Press Gorse for hostages and weapons both.

nodes.body: The confusion becomes a moving forest of poles, every point looking outward for a hunt that does not exist. You move through it like weather, unhurried, unremarkable, one more shape in a hall full of shapes. The fonts lamp ring glows a dozen strides ahead. And the waking words thread on through the noise, patient as rising water.

choices.label: Move on the rite.

nodes.body: The second call folds the rotunda into honest panic. Ranks collide, ringers lose the rite time, and Korvanes voice rises sharp for order in a tone hired men do not love. For the space of ten heartbeats the font stands unregarded, an island in a churning sea. You spend those heartbeats moving, and the effort costs you strength you no longer have to spare.

choices.label: Move on the rite through the churn.

nodes.body: The truth lands where Gorses fear already lived. He nods, slow, like a man agreeing with his own grandmother. A murmured order moves the captains like gears, and half the Reedcloaks simply turn and march for the stair while the masters voice climbs behind them, thin with disbelief. Gorses parting word comes over his shoulder in trade tongue. The door is yours, warden, and we were never here.

choices.label: Move on the rite as the ranks thin.

nodes.body: Plunder weighs against fear in a captains ledger, and the offer is taken with a spit and a handshake, both binding. Captains peel ranks away by the dozen, laden and cheerful. And the rotunda empties around a master too deep in his folio to count his own hirelings yet. You leave Gorse to his arithmetic and move on the font through the thinning ranks.

choices.label: Move on the rite as the ranks thin.

nodes.body: The call cracks on a cold lip, half hunt, half nothing, and every ear in the rotunda knows the difference. The side vaults are swept by ranks with torches, methodical as housekeeping, until the caller is found with the call still wet on his mouth. The Reedcloaks do not love call thieves, and the rite gains its audience after all.

An honor guard pole turns you back at the last stride, flat of the blade, almost courteous. Gorse shakes his head, slow, final. Some bargains stink worse than bilges, he says, and turns away. And the Coil master looks up from his folio at the disturbance with eyes like counting weights, and finds the sum of you standing there in the torchlight.

The ringers gallery is a balcony of hand bells and sweating backs, hammering the rite time out under a ceiling of torch smoke. Scaffold poles and old bell hooks hang overhead like a forest canopy of iron. A ring master paces the line with a tally cord, keeping time and discipline both, and neither kindly. The balcony runs the whole rotundas length, and at its far end a stair goes down to the floor and the rite.

choices.label: Cross behind the ringers under the scaffold.
Climb the bell hooks above the smoke.
Shoulder a hand bell and join the relief line.

nodes.body: You pace the crossing to the pealing, each stride swallowed whole by a bell. The ring masters cord snaps inches from your cheek as he disciplines a lagging ringer, and the ringer takes it without missing a stroke. You are a shadow between thunder and thunder, and the gallerys far stair comes in sight through the smoke, one bell line away.

choices.label: Take the far stair down to the floor.
Cut a scaffold lashing as you pass, for later.
Cross inside the ringers line where smoke is thickest.
Count the rite time from inside the bell line.

nodes.body: The bell hooks make a ladder for hands above the smoke, old iron sound as the day it was set. From up here the whole rite lies mapped below, ranks and font, and the doors seal beginning to glow along its carvings like a struck harp string. A hook line runs away and down from your perch, sagging gently, all the way to the pillar tops.

choices.label: Slide the hook line to the pillar shadow.
Climb higher into the smoke for a nearer line.

nodes.body: You take the far stair in bell time, down into floor silt and pole butts, a forest of legs all facing the wrong way. The lamp ring of the font glows ahead through the ranks, and nobody looks behind them, because the door is in front and the door is everything tonight. You thread the forest toward the light.

choices.label: Move on the rite.

nodes.body: One lashing parts with a knife whisper, and the bell scaffold holds its breath on a single knot now, a lever against the rite for whoever needs one later. You leave it hanging its quiet threat and take the far stair down to the floor. Where the bells walk your heartbeat down step by step ahead of your boots.

choices.label: Move on the rite with the scaffold in your pocket.
Cut a second lashing to be sure of it.

nodes.body: The hook line hums under your grip, and the slide is fast and blind through smoke that tastes of tallow and old brass. Your boots catch pillar stone in the deep shadow, jarring every joint you own. Somewhere ahead the waking words start, quiet as frost forming, and you spend the last of your wind just holding still to listen.

choices.label: Move on the rite.

nodes.body: The ring master turns at nothing, at instinct, and the tally cord points straight at your crossing like a compass finding north. The bells collapse into alarm peal. The balcony is a box with one door, and the door fills with pale robes, and the rite below never even pauses while the box is emptied of you.

The smoke runs thicker than any map of hooks. One handhold turns out to be only shadow, and the long reach into it ends the climb. The ringers never miss a beat below as you are carried out of the dark you fell from, and the smoke closes over the hooks as if no one had ever been foolish enough to trust them.

From the doors blind shadow the rite is close enough to touch. Korvane reads the waking words in a voice like water rising in a locked room. The Whisper sings to the font, and the seal carvings answer, glowing along the drowned door line by line. Stone grinds as the door stirs in its frame, an inch of dark opening on older dark, and the bell note swells toward floodtide like a tide finding its mark. The whole night narrows to one breath, and in that breath you choose what breaks first.

choices.label: Snatch the seal stone off the font mid verse.
Set Elowen and her chalk against the song.
Send Bram to overturn the cradle lamp ring.
Speak the masters name from the dark.
Wait one more verse for a perfect moment.

nodes.body: You snatch the seal stone off the font mid verse, and the seal answers with a note gone wrong, a scream of stone on stone. The song collapses around it. The stone bucks in your hands like a live thing, furious at the interruption, until the recoil tears it loose to skitter away across the flags, singing its broken note into the dark. And every eye in the rotunda turns to find what dared to touch it.

choices.label: Dive behind the pillars as the master turns.
Stand and face him over the fallen stone.
Chase the skittering stone across the open floor.

nodes.body: Elowens chalk circle flares like a struck match, and her small clear voice threads the Whispers song and snaps it clean in the middle, the old lines read as written against the inverted verse. The ringers lose the time. The seal glow gutters along the door like a candle in a doorway, dimming, failing. And the Whisper rounds on the dark with her teeth bared, hunting the counter chant with eyes that have sung to water for a hundred years and know exactly what a rival sounds like.

choices.label: Rush the cradle while the song is dead.
Leave Elowen to duel the singer and circle wide.
Add your own voice to the counter chant.

nodes.body: Bram hits the cradle like weather. The lamp ring goes over in a sheet of burning oil and sudden darkness, and the seal stone rolls loose across the flags with its low note wobbling. Coil voices climb over each other in the black, and the waking words stop mid syllable, cut off like a snuffed wick. Somewhere in the roaring dark, the stone is rolling, and everyone in the rotunda wants it.

choices.label: Hunt the rolling stone in the dark by its hum.
Scatter blind into the dark with everyone else.
Kick the burning oil across the woven mats.

nodes.body: You speak the masters name from the dark, just his name, and it lands in the rotunda like a dropped blade. Korvane stops mid word. A debtors vanity turns to find its audience, his eyes hunting the shadows while the seal glow steadies and dims behind him. Then his voice comes, pleasant as a letter opener. Whoever knows him well enough to interrupt, he says, knows enough to be useful, and he offers partnership to the dark at large.

choices.label: Refuse him with silence and move on the font.
Step into the light and accept his bargain.

nodes.body: Gray green light hammers the pillar where you stood a breath ago, and the stone hisses like a quenched link. The master advances on the fallen seal stone with his folio held open before him like a shield, reading as he walks. Behind him the rotunda folds into rout as the bells die rank by rank. And the night comes apart into running shadows and one patient man in dry robes.

choices.label: Circle to put the stone between you and him.

nodes.body: You face off over the fallen stone, his folio open, your iron out, and the half woken door grinding its slow inch behind you both. Everything in the rotunda that is not already running stops to watch. Korvane studies you the way he studied the font, like a debt he intends to collect. And the seals broken note hangs in the air between you, waiting to be claimed.

choices.label: Hold his eyes and buy the party position.

nodes.body: You rush the cradle through the dead song, bowl the Whisper aside into her own incense, and knock the seal stone spinning from its cradle to the flags. The stone lands singing its wrong note, loud and ruinous. Behind you Korvanes voice goes from water to steam in the space of one word, and the dark fills with gray green light looking for something to punish.

choices.label: Turn with the stone between you and the master.

nodes.body: You hunt the hum through burning oil light and screaming shadow, down on your knuckles among running boots. One hand closes on cold stone at the same instant a soft shoe pins the other end to the flags. You look up the dry robe to the eyes above it, and for a moment the master and you are joined by a seal that neither of you owns yet. Then you wrench it free, and the effort costs you the last easy strength you had.

choices.label: Wrench it free and give ground.

nodes.body: The perfect moment arrives one verse too late. The seal sings itself open, and the drowned door swings wide on a cold that puts out torches. Something crowned and dripping stands in the dark of it as the waking words end. The temple empties in a rout that carries the party out like flood wrack, alive and unlistened to, and green light burns over the Greymarsh all winter, visible from Barrowmere on clear nights, patient as debt.

The gray green light finds you a half step slow, a hammer of cold that makes no sound at all. The rotunda tilts up to meet you, stone and torch glow and dark. The last thing that reaches you is the song starting over somewhere far away, patient, unhurried, as if you had never interrupted anything.

The Whispers answering shriek is older than her wards, and there are teeth in the incense before you can turn. Shell charms snap tight around your wrists and your words alike, binding both. The rite resumes behind you as you are dragged to the side gallery, and you watch the rest from your knees, gagged with knotted gut, one witness the song did not need to silence twice.

There are too many hands in your own darkness. Pale eyes were always better in the dark than yours, and the dark you made belongs to them the moment you make it. The seal stone rolls to a soft shoed stop somewhere out of reach, and the lamp ring is relit around four bound guests of the rite, seated politely where the view is best.

You accept the bargain in front of witnesses who melt away wisely. And Korvanes smile sets like a signature drying. The fine print arrives as the coral sigil at his throat warms. An apprenticeship of errands and debts and never quite leaving, renewable at his pleasure, which is eternal. The company walks out of the Greymarsh without you while the master finds you endlessly, endlessly useful.

The whole night turns on this floor. The seal stone lies on the flags between you and the master, its song fallen back to silence. The drowned door stands stuck half a hand open, and something gray gropes patient at the gap, in no hurry at all. Reedcloak ranks rout up every stair with their bells dead. Korvane measures you over his open folio, unhurried, doing arithmetic of his own. And your body tells you plainly, in the language of burning legs and borrowed breath, exactly how much it has left to spend.

choices.label: Stand, take the stone, and end the Coil masters night.
You are spent, dive for the stone and run for your lives.

nodes.body: You make the last stand standing. Bram plants himself a wall on your left, Sable becomes a rumor on your right, and Elowen chalks fast behind you, whispering her letters. The seal stone is snatched to your chest, singing low against your ribs like a cat that does not want to be held. Korvane comes on with gray green light gathering in his free hand, slow as weather deciding, and the space between you empties of everything except the next move.

choices.label: Strike the folio from his hands.
Give ground and let the groping gap have him.
Rush him bodily with Bram, eel line ready.
Race him to the stone barehanded, speed against sorcery.

nodes.body: Sables thrown pommel and your lunge arrive together, and the folio goes spinning from his grip, lead covers shedding pages like a shot bird. The gathering light dies unspent in his hand. And the creditor is suddenly only a man in dry robes, backing away from four angry young faces and one dwarf with an ax, his voice bargaining down through threats to offers to silence. You let him run into the dark and see to your own instead.

choices.label: Let him run into the dark and see to your own.

nodes.body: You give ground a step at a time, drawing him on, until the cold of the gap breathes on your neck and you sidestep like a dancer leaving the floor. The gray hand closes instead on the voice that woke it, gentle as a debt collector. Korvanes last word is the start of a bargain no one stays to hear out. Then the door grinds shut again on its own hunger, and the rotunda is very quiet.

choices.label: Stand very still until the stone is quiet.
Crowd him into the gap yourself, hand against hand.

nodes.body: The rush is a fen answer to sorcery. Bram takes the gray light on his shield boss with a grunt like a struck bell, and then the master is borne down under honest weight and eel line, wrapped to the chin, trussed with his own chained folio for a pillow. He curses in three languages and then stops, saving his breath, already planning. You haul him up between you and signal the way out, spending strength you will want later.

choices.label: Haul him up and signal the way out.

nodes.body: The scramble is lost by a fingertip. The master wins his own weight in vanity back, stone and folio both, and the gray light herds the party up the dead stair like sheep before a storm. Behind you his laughter closes like a ledger. The temple is lost, the raising only postponed until the next floodtide, and the road home is long and full of the arithmetic of failure.

One step too near the patient gap, and the gray grip finds your shoulder instead of his, cold beyond argument, beyond struggle. The rotunda goes far away and small, like a lit window seen from a night mere. The last thing over the threshold of hearing is the seal starting its lullaby again, and this verse of it is for you.

The dive takes the seal stone on bleeding knuckles, and there is no strength left in you for masters, only for running. Bram scatters a brazier behind you, Sable calls the turns ahead, and Elowen throws her last chalk to flare blinding white at the masters feet. The temple above wakes like a kicked hive, every stair filling with noise. The stone hums against your ribs, and your legs ask, reasonably, how far.

choices.label: Run for the sluice line and the drain.
Run for the choir ways and out the long dark.
Turn at bay in the antechamber arch.
Cut past the half open door itself, the shortest line.
Climb against the rout and take the bell tower out.

nodes.body: You run the sluice line on fumes, sending the long heron call ahead down the drain. Iron grates and cold water, hands hauling hands through the dark, eleven freed voices counting each other off ahead of you. The ditch takes you all in a tangle under the last of the moon, soaked and shaking and alive. While the temple above roars at its own emptiness like a robbed strongbox.

choices.label: Count heads in the ditch and move.
Stop to wrap the humming stone mid run.

nodes.body: The choir ways swallow the party one by one, and the coral slot sighs shut on the masters curses, cutting them off mid syllable. The stone passages run dark and patient under the mire, older than the Coil, older than the grange. Elowens last candle stub lights spans measured out by drowned men, and you walk the long dark out with the stone quiet against your back, as if it approves.

choices.label: Walk the long dark out.

nodes.body: The antechamber arch is a fine place for a last stand and a poor one for living. Poles fill it end to end while gray light seals the far arch at your backs. The seal stone is prised from your arms almost gently, a valuable recovered, and the pens close over the company after all, with the rite to be rebuilt by the next floodtide and a much better guarded font.

The shortcut past the half open door is a memory before it finishes. The gray reach is faster than tired legs, and the cold takes the sound out of the world like a hand over a candle. The seal sings you down into the dark it guards, and the song, at least, is gentle.

The way out opens with the stone singing low and the master finished. Above, the temple is already emptying, Reedcloak ranks streaming for dry land with their bargain dead. The pens give up their people into the gray terrace, and Marta counts fen folk aloud like a woman counting her own heartbeats. Whin sleeps at last on Brams shoulder, small and heavy as a good deed. And the east shows gray as the company walks out the broken ring wall, unopposed, into the ordinary miracle of morning.

choices.label: Take the road home to Barrowmere.
Parade the bound master once around the terrace first.

nodes.body: In the quiet after the door, with retreat bells sounding far above, Gorse himself waits at the antechamber arch with his honor guard. He looks a long time at the shut door, and at the stone in your arms, and says his grandmother was right about everything. The bargain is done. The Reedcloaks pole for dry land at dawn, and the prisoners walk free, his word on it, which tonight is worth more than any coin in the temple.

choices.label: March everyone home under a hired captains strange safe conduct.

nodes.body: The ditch gives onto the open mire, and eleven freed souls and four spent ones move hummock to hummock while the temple burns torchlight at its own shadows behind you. Marta leads by ways only peat folk know, cut and causeway and dry line. The stone rides wrapped in a cloak on your back, still humming its one low note into your spine. And the last miles walk themselves into a dawn the color of pewter.

choices.label: Walk the last miles into dawn.

nodes.body: The choir ways give out at last through a fox thicket door on a dry esker two miles south of everything. Stars hang overhead like spilled salt. The long way home begins on aching legs, and the stone goes quiet at last against your back, as if it knows it is walking away from the door it sings to. Nobody talks for the first mile, and there is nothing in any of you but breath and luck.

choices.label: Take the long way home.

nodes.body: Barrowmere at midmorning, and the company walks up the Kings Road with eleven souls behind them and the second seal in an iron banded box beside the first. Hedda meets you at the gate with the town at her back, and Reed grins from Wecks cart like a boy who bet right. Fifty crowns are counted on the marshals table, and the Brass Lantern toasts its second door shut until the candles drown. In the quiet after, Hedda spreads Korvanes folio where the water failed to ruin it. One line survives, and she reads it twice before she says it aloud. The third sleeps under the mountains.

It is the strangest homecoming yet. A Reedcloak honor guard halts at the third milestone and watches eleven freed fen folk and four ragged heroes walk the last stretch alone. Gorses parting gift is a reed tied in a sailors knot, laid on your map over the mountain passes, with a warning growled in trade tongue. The pale ones buy ponies there too. Hedda pays fifty crowns to a company that made a hired captain keep his word, and the taproom hangs the knotted reed over the door beside the chiefs arrow.

Dawn comes up over the open mire, and Barrowmere rings its bells at the sight of the column. Eleven souls are delivered footsore and laughing at nothing, the way the rescued laugh. The second seal is set beside the first on Heddas table, and the two boxes sing faintly to each other until they are parted to opposite ends of the room. Marta signs her peat bond closed with a steady hand. And the marshal reads the one legible folio line twice before she says it aloud. The third sleeps under the mountains.

The long way round ends four days late, through the south fords, and the town is half into mourning bands when the company walks in whole. The bells find their voices one by one. The second seal is delivered with esker burrs still in everyones hair, and Hedda docks nothing from the fifty crowns. She adds a map besides, rolled and sealed, because the Coils ledger ran north into the mountains, and someone with a brass lantern is going to have to follow it.

The town square holds more fen than usual. Refugees camp by the well trough with their lives packed in two baskets each, quiet people made quieter, and the well talk runs all one direction, bells over the mire, pale buyers, a sister taken. A small vigil candle burns on the trough rim in front of a fen wife with a face like held weather. Everyone in the square knows who you are. Nobody asks for anything out loud.

choices.label: Set a candle at the fen wifes vigil.
Draw out the off duty sergeant.
Get pulled into mending the trough wall.

nodes.body: You set your own candle beside the fen wifes without a word, and she watches the two flames lean together. Her sister was taken off a crannog in the night, boat and all. When she finally speaks, it is to give you something, the temples old name, the one her grandmother used. The Choir Under the Water, she says. They did not always take people, and once they kept something in.

choices.label: Take the temples old name to the inn.

nodes.body: The duty sergeant is off shift and talkative for the price of an ale. Three south reports this month, he says, all mentioning one bell that does not stop. The watch is forbidden past the fords, not by courage but by the towns purse, and it grinds him. Somebody with a commission and a famous lantern, he says, staring into his mug, could go where wages cannot.

choices.label: Take the bell reports to the inn.

nodes.body: The trough wall wants mending, and you are famous enough to be borrowed. One stone becomes a course, a course becomes an afternoon, and the whole square finds one more small job for the heroes of Harrowstone. By the time the wall stands true the south window is shut for the day, and the fen keeps its schedule while yours drains into mortar.

The oddments cart at the square edge sells fen salvage among the tin, and one piece stops you cold. A green glass float, etched with a coiled snake, the etching fresher than the glass. The trader sells it cheap and will not say where it came from, except that he wants it gone before dark, and that the man who sold it to him would not touch it bare handed.

choices.label: Buy the snake etched float and go in.
Follow the trader into the alley to haggle.

nodes.body: The haggle in the alley turns out to have partners, and the lesson costs your purse and most of your dignity. The watch takes statements with a straight face while the afternoon light drains away. The float and the trader are both gone south by dusk. And the company starts its famous expedition by reporting a robbery, which the fiddler had better never learn.

The drover table at the Lantern is loud with plans tonight. Two fen born hands want to ride south with billhooks and grief, tonight, now, and they want the famous company to lead them. It is brave and it is doomed and everyone at the table knows both, and they ask anyway, because their people are in the pens and yours came home.

choices.label: Cool the ride and take their fen lore instead.
Give the vengeance ride your blessing.

nodes.body: You give the vengeance ride your blessing, and grief with billhooks goes south at midnight. It comes back at dawn lashed over two saddles, alive by inches, and the company spends its departure week mending what its word helped break. The fen did not even notice the blow it parried. That is the part the drover table cannot forgive, and the part you cannot forget.

The fiddler has a new verse about Harrowstone, and half of it is wrong and all of it is kind. In the song there are twelve of you, the door is a dragon, and nobody is ever afraid. The taproom watches the real company listen to the tall version of itself, and Pell watches the company. And the song ends on your name rhymed with lantern, which does not rhyme, and gets the biggest cheer of the night.

choices.label: Take the song with grace and find Pell.

nodes.body: You stand Bram an ale at the forge wagon and talk shield work, shop to shop, the marsh mentioned only sideways. He shows you the new boss he has hammered, a lantern in relief, and pretends it is just geometry. The ale goes down. The fen comes up on its own, the way it was always going to, and Bram brings it up himself, which was the whole strategy.

choices.label: Let the dwarf talk himself aboard.
Push too hard when he hesitates.

nodes.body: Sable counters with her own wager, because of course she does. She walks the roof ridge blind, both ends, if you stake the commissions first week against her letter. The street gathers before the terms are even settled, the way streets do. She ties the blindfold herself, tight, showing off, and the ridge line waits under the morning sky like a drawn blade.

choices.label: Stake the week and watch her win it.
Welsh when she names the stake.

nodes.body: She names the stake and you welsh on a public roof, and Sables face closes like a shutter in a storm. The scout keeps the city offer after all, which everyone watching understands completely. The company that crosses the fen without its eyes learns at the first patrol exactly what got welshed away on that roof. And the letter never even had to win.

You put the question to Elowen herself, plainly, while her aunt listens from the doorway. Does she want the fen or the shop. Elowen sets down the jar she is holding, thinks the way she thinks, completely, and says the shop will still be here when the bells stop. It is the aunt who looks away first, and the packing starts that afternoon.

choices.label: Back her answer against her aunt.
Let the aunt shut the question down.

nodes.body: Hedda answers the redcloak question without being asked twice. Hired by a southern purse she cannot trace, papers in order, route filed at the gate. Her advice comes flat as her map table. Let them run ahead and read what the marsh does to them, she says, and professionals learn from other peoples tuition. The south road takes you an hour behind two red cloaks and a lot of noise.

choices.label: Take the fords road behind the redcloaks.

nodes.body: The stile shepherd has kept this gate a lifetime, and he names every wagon that passed this month, market carts and wool wains and the heavy gray ones that passed only at night, six of them, riding low. His dogs would not bark at the gray wagons, he says, and that is the part that keeps him up. Dogs bark at strangers. They go quiet for storms.

choices.label: Head down to the fen edge road.
Stay for the shepherds last word.

nodes.body: The shepherd gives his last word over the gate like a coin he has saved. When the bell under the water sings twice, get to stone and stay there. His grandmother had it from hers, and hers from the drowned parish, and he cannot say what it means. He can say that his family kept it two hundred years and kept their feet dry, and the gate swings open on that.

choices.label: Carry the warning to the fen edge.

nodes.body: The boggy cut below the stile goes exactly as the toll camp hoped, hip deep in ten strides and laughed at from the wall. The dogs owners haul you out for double the toll, drying fees extra, and send the famous company back down the road wringing out its dignity. The stile stays theirs. The story, unfortunately, travels.

Wecks eel boy fights the pots alone at the stern, so you show him the double hitch and haul the last mile beside him. He pays the only way boys pay, in everything he knows. The crow trees, the drowned chapel, which stage planks are rotten, and the pale customers who chartered the other barge, who stood in the middle of the deck, he says, like the water might reach up.

choices.label: Put ashore at the last bend.

nodes.body: You doze at the gunwale mid river, and the pot line snags the boom around in one lazy swing. The brown current takes its chance without malice, the way rivers always have, and the Greymarsh crossing ends before the fen edge. In the wet dark under a barge, with Wecks grief the last sound over the water.

You count the wheel marks properly, kneeling in the rut mud like a priest of traffic. Nine wagons in three nights, all riding heavy south and coming back light. A supply line, read straight out of the ground. Somebody is feeding an operation in the deep fen, feeding it well, and paying carters to do it at night, and night wages mean questions are not included.

choices.label: Take the supply line reading to the ford.
Hail the shepherd across the sedge.

nodes.body: A carrland shepherd answers your hail across the sedge and trades the old fords directions for news of the market at the fords. Fair dealing both ways. Then he adds, unasked, that his dogs will not go south of the crow trees anymore, not for whistles, not for supper. He does not ask where you are going, and he looks at the dogs, and then at you, and wishes you a dry road.

choices.label: Make the fen edge road by his directions.

nodes.body: You follow the crows low over the reed bank, and they teach you what they know for free. They wheel wide of one green patch, every circuit, a hole in the sky exactly the size of something alive. When the wind drops, the patch coughs. Wet, small, human. The crows resume their patient orbit, professionals themselves, waiting for the fen to settle the question.

choices.label: Close on the coughing reeds.

nodes.body: You walk the wreck perimeter slow, and the sedge gives up a dropped grapple, good iron, fen made, flung wide in some scuffle. Then the high reeds on the south rise move against the wind, exactly once, exactly the way reeds do not. The wreck has a watcher, and the watcher has a post. And the post has a blind side, if you walk the long way.

choices.label: Slip to the reed bank the long way.
Wave the company forward in the open.

nodes.body: You wave the company forward in the open with the south rise still unchecked, and the rise answers. Gray hoods come out of the reeds on both banks at once, polite as ushers, poles level. The company is walked south trussed beside the family it came to find, and the watcher on the rise collects a bounty for an afternoons patience.

Elowens fen herbs go to work on Reeds arrow crease, aunt Aldous list proving its worth in the first mile. The boy watches her work with a professionals respect, then teaches the marsh calls before he is even steady on his feet. Heron, plover, all clear, because debts get paid promptly in the fen, and the calls are the coin he has.

choices.label: Walk the guide to Saltmire hamlet.

nodes.body: The old burial mound rises above Saltmire ringed with rag offerings, fen folk keeping faith with whatever sleeps in it. A low stone passage opens into its dark side, half curtained in roots, and the mounds lee breaks the building wind like a wall. The hamlet says the old dead are good neighbors. The hamlet also does not sleep up here, you notice.

choices.label: Sleep in the mounds sheltered lee.
Go back down and square the gear.
Squeeze into the stone passage itself.

nodes.body: You sleep in the mounds lee out of the wind, and the old dead prove good neighbors after all. No dreams, no bells, just deep black rest while the gale spends itself on the other side of the stones. The company wakes strong with the mist still down, and somebody has left a rag knot on Brams shield boss, which the fen wives say is a blessing, and Bram does not remove.

choices.label: Take the pole trail in the mist.

nodes.body: The stone passage narrows past argument, and pride pushes one shoulder farther than sense. Wedged fast in the old dark, you spend half a day being dug out by the hamlet with ropes and tallow and commentary. The old dead take no offense, the headwoman says. The schedule does, and so does everyone who heard about it, forever.

Reed rigs his decoy trick at the side channel, a weighted splash on a peeled withy, sprung from cover. The splash goes down the wrong water loud as a diving otter, and every reed bank ahead leans to listen to the wrong place. The trail ahead stands briefly deaf, and Reed grins like a boy who has cheated the fen at its own game, once, and knows better than to try twice.

choices.label: Move on the crannog while reeds listen wrong.
Wade the long way while the trick holds.
Add a marsh call to sell the decoy.

nodes.body: You add a marsh call to sell the decoy, and pitch it a shade too true. A real plover answers first, flattered. Then the picket that hunts by plovers answers the plover. The decoy catches its own thrower in a closing ring of gray, and Reeds best trick finishes the night working for the other side.

A drowned tollhouse stands mid causeway with its roof still above water, slates loose as old teeth, and its attic offers a dry route over the patrol narrows for anyone light footed enough to want it. The joists are sound, Bram judges, tapping one through the gable gap. The slates are the argument. Below, a patrol pair works the narrows, and their voices carry up through the floor like talk through a drum.

choices.label: Cross the attic joist to joist.
Pause at the gable and listen first.
Hurry the loose slates at a trot.

nodes.body: You cross the attic joist to joist, patrol voices passing directly under the floor, complaints about wet boots and floodtide wages. The far gable gives onto a dry drop behind their backs, and the causeway stones take you on south while the pair argues on toward the north narrows, none the wiser. The tollhouse kept its last toll for you, and charged nothing.

choices.label: Drop behind the patrol and push on.

nodes.body: You pause at the gable and let the patrol talk beneath you, and passwords trade below, plain as market chatter.
