Floodtide for the challenge, coil and rise for the answer, changed at every watch, and this watch is young. Words worth their weight in gray cloth, if used before the change, and worth a noose after. The attic crossing finishes with the toll paid in patience.

choices.label: Take the words and cross the attic.
Use the password on the next patrol.

nodes.body: You hurry the loose slates at a trot, and one goes off the roof like a thrown plate, spinning, unmissable. The splash is worse. The tollhouse attic is surrounded before the ripples settle, patrol pairs converging from both narrows, and the dry route ends with a wet arrest and a slow pole ride to the temple under guard.

You wade the arch line beneath the causeway road bed, water to the chest, boots finding the old invert stones one arch at a time. Patrols pass overhead like weather, boot heels knocking the stones above your hood. The dark under the arches is green and dripping and thoroughly indifferent, and it hides you the whole cold way.

choices.label: Wade the arch line under the road.

nodes.body: The last arches go numb and slow, cold banked so deep in the bones it stops feeling like weather and starts feeling like furniture. But the ring wall arrives under the patrols very feet, its broken stretch a body length from the last arch. And the company climbs out of the water into temple shadow having never once been looked at.

choices.label: Come up cold at the ring wall.

nodes.body: The old pilgrim stair cuts up the esker flank, steps worn to saucers by centuries of the faithful, jackdaws nesting in every socket now. The birds are the lock on this door. One startled daw is a shout, a whole colony is an alarm bell with wings, and the stair asks quiet of every boot, saucer by saucer, all the way to the crest.

choices.label: Climb the stair a boot at a time.
Push the pace past the nests.

nodes.body: The stair tops out without a single bird startled, which Sable declares a personal record and Reed declares luck. The watch platform stands below now, seen from above for once, and its blind side lies open like a gift. Even its watch fire helps, night blinding the sentry to everything outside its circle. Including the company walking quietly around the rim of his world.

choices.label: Take the platforms blind side.

nodes.body: One saucer step lands proud instead of quiet, and the whole jackdaw city rises in a screaming wheel that can be seen from the temple and probably from Barrowmere. Every watch eye on the esker turns to the birds, then under them. The stair empties you back down the flank at a run, and the road is burned for the night, if not the week.

You walk the crest through the dark hours instead, cold and slow, and the watch fires map themselves for you by their own glow. Every light a post, every gap a road. The temple terrace arrives with the night half spent and the company half frozen, but the whole esker line lies behind you read like a ledger, and nobody reads back.

choices.label: Walk the crest dark to the terrace.
Check the pilgrims cache stone on the way.

nodes.body: You tap the drain wall junction by junction, ear to the brick, and the sound leads true. Dull where earth backs the wall, ringing where the flooded main run parallels. A mason trick of Brams, worth the delay, and it walks you to the main run by ear alone, the lantern saved for the dark that will actually need it.

choices.label: Follow the hollow line to the main run.

nodes.body: The old sluice dam machinery survives at the mid drain, gates seized a century, a great iron wheel furred with rust. It still turns a hand width with four sets of arms on it, and a hand width, Bram calculates, drops the flooded run beyond by a boot depth. The wheel groans like a waking grandfather. The water listens, and begins, reluctantly, to go down.

choices.label: Wade the dropped run to the cistern.
Force the seized gate wheel further.
Watch the otter run the dam first.

nodes.body: You force the seized wheel past its last honest inch, and the old gate lets go all at once, a century of pent water deciding in one heartbeat. The surge takes the drain and the company both back the way they came, spat into the alder brake soaked and coughing. And the sluice spends the rest of the night roaring its opinion of impatience to the whole fen.

You whistle to test the echo, and the echo answers true from ahead, and then again from behind, which echoes do not do. The drain has carried your position both directions like a good servant, and lanterns close from either end at a walk. Unhurried, because the drain has no third exit and everybody in it knows that now.

The char kilns read like a ledger for anyone who grew up banking fires. Fired in rotation, banked by a paid hand, the newest ash still holding warmth in its heart. Somebody tends this line on wages, which means shifts, which means the straight run beyond has a schedule. You take its measure and take the run between tendings, walking through a strangers workday like a rumor.

choices.label: Take the straight run past the kilns.

nodes.body: The rival cutters at their fire turn out to be Saltmire folk. Cut out of their own galleries by Coil silver and sour as green ale about it. Their reeve is a knuckly old hand called Coalfinger, who listens to your business, spits professionally, and names a price for the back ways, not coin, sweat. There is a windlass wants turning, and dignity has no place in a peat cut.

choices.label: Pay Coalfingers price for the back ways.
Haggle him down one measure too far.

nodes.body: Coalfingers back ways run honest after all, older cuts threading under the new work, and his directions prove exact to the prop. The price gets paid at the windlass, an hour of shoulder work raising his seasons char, and the last shaft opens inside sight of the ring wall. Fair dealing, fen fashion, and both sides leave richer.

choices.label: Come up the cutters shaft at the wall.

nodes.body: You haggle the blind womans son of a reeve down one measure too far, and Coalfingers face shuts like a tool chest. The Coils standing bounty beats a strangers short arm, he decides, and the back ways lead exactly where he is paid to lead them. Up a cutters shaft into a ring of waiting tallow light and leveled mattocks.

The digger bluff holds right up until the foreman asks which crew, friendly as a toll keeper. And there is no safe answer in the fen to a question with a list behind it. The gallery fills with tallow light and lifted mattocks, and the hired hands earn their Coil bonus the easy way, four strangers at a time.

The terrace tackle shed is banked for the night, all shadow and cooling iron and the smell of quenched links. A path threads its racks toward the inner gate, lined both sides with chain and hooks. And one smith sleeps stretched on the bench with a hammer for a pillow, snoring like a bellows with a leak. The racks are close, the shadows are deep, and the sleeper holds the only alarm that matters in his dreaming hand.

choices.label: Thread the racks past the sleeper.
Tiptoe the shadow side of the bench.
Hurry it and trust the snoring.

nodes.body: Your elbow finds the one hanging chain in all that dark, and iron applause rings off every rack in the shed. The smith comes awake mid snore with his hammer already moving, and the shed door fills with torches before the echoes settle. Caught among the tools of somebody elses trade, you are marched to the pens with the applause still ringing.

The terrace well head stands by the platform, windlass rope sound as the day it was wound, drum greased, handle willing. You ride it down past wet stone courses into the dark, and at half depth a ledge opens exactly where the warden marks promised. The spring gallery, a forgotten service way running under the inner gate, dry as old bone and quiet as the inside of a bell.

choices.label: Ride the windlass down to the ledge.
Swing wide to skip the last courses.

nodes.body: The windlass pawl shears at half depth with a crack like a knuckle, and the rope screams through the drum. The bucket ledge arrives hard and wet, knocking the wind out of the whole night. The long climb back up delivers you into waiting torchlight, wrung out and dripping, and the well keeps your dignity as a deposit while the guards collect the rest.

The traded password gets spent one watch too late. The challenge comes, floodtide, and your answer lands in a silence with edges on it, because the countersign changed at the change of watch. The narrow fills from both ends while the stale word hangs in the air, and the causeway collects its toll after all, in full.

The packs go up the scarp first, tossed, and a hand goes out to catch a strap instead of stone. Divided grip, running gravel, and the arithmetic finishes itself. The scarp foot collects what the years were always going to collect, and the esker road goes on without you, high and dry and indifferent.

The third mouth breathes cold like the right one, and lies. It spirals into dead grange brick, hours of it, the count gone to guesswork and the oil going to smoke. The sluice finally surrenders the company back to daylight in the alder brake, a day poorer and a temper shorter, with the marks unread and the fen unimpressed.

The deep unpropped cut skips the crew and finds the hills patience instead. The roof talks in ticks and settles, and then a fall behind seals the way back, soft and total. The only door out is the diggers own, opened from their side on their schedule, and the waiting in the dark before it opens is its own sentence.

The reed sledge rides past the inner arch without slowing, and by the time the wrongness of that lands, it is parking at the hound pen gate. Pitchforks come first at the pens, as pitchforks always do, and the reeds give up their stowaways to a ring of grinning handlers and very interested hounds. The ride to the pens is short, and everyone itches the whole way.

The brine vats thread rim to rim, salt fog rolling off their open tops in slow curtains. Eyes sting, boots stay silent on the wet stone. And the pens gallery arrives through the last curtain with the whole approach made invisible by somebody elses pickling. Elowen fills a spare skin from the nearest vat on the way past, on a theory about hound noses she declines to explain yet.

choices.label: Come out at the pens gallery.
Fill a skin with brine for the hounds noses.

nodes.body: Sable goes to the hound pen with a wink and a strip of dried eel. And two hundred heartbeats later the terrace above erupts in honest dog joy at a sprung gate. Boots thunder the wrong way across the pavement, whistles and curses and the delighted baying of hounds who cannot believe their luck. The pens stand suddenly unwatched, and the gift of noise is yours for exactly as long as the chase runs.

choices.label: Use the noise and open the grates now.
Wait out the chase one round more.

nodes.body: You wait out the noise one round too long, and the kennel chase loops back early with the hounds leading it home. Wet noses find the pens corner unerringly, joyful and thorough, and the whole wing arrives behind them at a run. The hounds are extremely pleased with everyone involved. The feeling is not mutual, and the pens close over the rescue like water.

The drain grate in the pens floor lifts on Brams fingers a knuckle at a time, patient as tide. Below runs a dry way, low and close, threading under the cell row itself. You go along it tapping softly beneath each grate, mapping which cells breathe and which stand empty. And the pens answer in shifting straw and held coughs, an inventory taken from underneath while the warder dreams over his keys.

choices.label: Come up inside and start the freeing.

nodes.body: You split the party against every instinct you own. You and Sable to the gallery, light and fast. Bram and Elowen holding the pens, shield and craft. Two smaller shadows instead of one loud one, and the meeting point named twice over so nobody can mishear it. Bram grips your shoulder once, hard, which is a whole speech in dwarf. Then the wing swallows the halves of you separately.

choices.label: Take the gallery stairs with Sable.

nodes.body: You seat the vault lid a finger too true, and the dark under it goes close, and closer, and spends itself. Whin cries at last at the shut black, one thin wail through stone, and the lid comes up into rush light and leveled poles. The hiding becomes the finding, and the pens refill in the gray hours, one cough richer than before.

You whistle the pens an all clear a shade too tunefully, and the warders own whistle answers it from the chair, which was the real signal all along. The pens corner becomes the best lit place in the wing before you can lower your fingers from your lips. Old traps in old prisons, and the oldest one is confidence.

You light the pen lamps to work faster, and the light works faster than you do. It shines like a festival through every grate and crack in the wing, and the passage guards come to it moth sure, poles first. The freeing is undone by its own light, and the dark you should have kept comes back down over everything like a dropped lid.

The loose slab looks like the stand up end and is not. You rise through it into a lit gallery instead of a dark one, shoulders first, exactly as the working passage crosses with torches and a full shift. There is a long moment where everyone simply looks at everyone. Then the gallery does its arithmetic, and the pens gain a tenant who arrived through the floor.

The stacked stores by the hall door make a wall of flour casks and eel barrels, and a crawlspace through them is a matter of moving the right ones. Two ways offer themselves, the patient way, cask by cask with every weight tested. Or the fast way between serving shifts, trusting the stack to hold its own arithmetic while you burrow.

choices.label: Shift the casks the patient way.
Shift them fast between shifts.
Climb the stack and take the top route.

nodes.body: The crawlspace opens cask by cask, a channel through the stores wide as a shoulder and dark as a pocket. You go through it on elbows with the feast roaring one plank away, close enough to smell the mutton. And the vault side takes you out behind the halls back, unseen, smelling faintly of flour and entirely of victory.

choices.label: Slip through to the vault side.

nodes.body: The top route across the stacked stores finds the one cask lid soft as bread. And the fall through flour and pickled eels is loud enough to season the whole halls evening. The crossing ends in laughter, alarm, and brine, roughly in that order, and the company is collected from the wreckage smelling like a chandlers fire and marched to the pens.

The vaults barred inner door hides a strongroom of Coil pay silver, week wages for the whole operation racked in canvas rolls. The bar is heavy but liftable, and time is the only real price. Silver pays no debts tonight, Sable observes, and information does, and the service stair is right there. The bar stays down or comes up. The night keeps its own ledger.

choices.label: Leave the silver and take the stair.
Lift the bar and try the strongroom.

nodes.body: The strongroom bar lifts, and the strongroom disagrees. An inner gate falls with a clang built by suspicious men, sealing the silver and its visitor together like a specimen jar. The tally keepers find you at shift change, politely furious. And the Coil pays no bounty for this one, because you delivered yourself.

The forge annex exits through the quench steam, a white blind curtain crossed by memory and count. Heat on both cheeks, the door found by the seventh stride exactly where the count said, and the steam closes behind you like a drawn curtain. The smith hammers on, three blows and a breath, unaware his weather was ever weather for anyone else.

choices.label: Cross the steam by count and go.
Grope for the tongs rack mid steam.

nodes.body: You grope for the tongs rack mid steam, and the quench cloud parts on the smiths own face a hand span from yours. He is not a fighter, and the forge bell is right there anyway, and he hammers it with a professionals economy before your eyes even clear. The annex fills, the wing follows, and the steam gets the last word.

One tablet becomes five, and the lead stack is heavier and the hour later than either felt. The vault door closes on a shift change with the reader still inside, and morning delivers the inventory, four strangers found among the lots, priced and processed with everything else. The tablets, at least, get read. In the pens, from memory, aloud.

The long tongs come along on farm habit, hung at the belt like it is haying season, and two passages later they ring on a doorframe like a cowbell. The wing comes to see which cow. There is no good answer to that in any language, and the pens take delivery of one more lesson about traveling light.

You slick the store floor with oil as insurance, and the insurance collects early. A Coil hand goes down loud with his lamp, the spill lights, and the store alarms a full act ahead of schedule. The wing rises around the fire like a struck nest, and the company is taken at the drain mouth, smoked out and singed, with the plan burned along with the mats.

The incense smoke thickens toward the den until eyes stream and the world goes soft at the edges. Elowen soaks a kerchief in the flooded margin and passes it down the line, fen field craft against temple perfume, and the coughing that wants to happen gets bought off breath by breath. The last curtain of smoke stands ahead, and the song is on the other side of it.

choices.label: Press on to the last curtain.

nodes.body: You cross the last incense curtain low and slow, kerchief tight, the den glow blooming ahead through the haze. The cough sits under your ribs like a live thing under a lid, pressing, patient, and you hold it down stride by stride. The smoke thins, and the song sharpens, and the den opens ahead, and the lid holds.

choices.label: Slip into the den low and quiet.
Clear your throat once, softly.

nodes.body: The smothered cough wins on the third curtain, one small human sound in all that incense, and the song stops mid phrase. The stopping is the loudest thing the grotto has ever done. Shell wards pick up where the song left off, chattering the alarm passage to passage, and the incense parts on pale faces arriving from every direction at once.

One racked bone flute comes to your lips at the margins edge, and Elowens counter note goes out into the incense, a wardens interval she swears by, breath measured out like coin. The song ahead stumbles on the interval, just slightly, like a walker finding a stair where none was. It is not much. In a temple built of singing, it does not need to be.

choices.label: Carry the counter note to the den.
Blow it a half tone braver.

nodes.body: The counter note goes out a half tone flat, and the song swallows it whole and hands it back as a question. The question has your position in it. The den empties toward the answer with the flute still at your lips, and the last thing the margin offers is the observation that music, like tide, forgives nothing.

You offer your name in parley as surety, and the Whisper explains gently that names are not debts, they are property, and hers now. The wards close like a purse on the word. You leave the grotto with the key unwon and something harder to name unowned, and the song uses your name once, softly, as you go, just to show it can.

One look back from the den door, and the tide bowls surface meets your eyes across the whole room. The singers smeared hush sign flares on the lintel above you, and the room rings you in place, gently, completely, until hands arrive to collect what the sign is holding. Manners, the Whisper says to her bowl, not even turning. Manners.

The hush mark gets thickened once too often, and the chalk stub spends itself and stays behind, glowing faintly on the arch. Elowens kit is one charm short at the exact hour the rite floor will ask for all of them. The visit ends there, on arithmetic, and the long way back out is spent listening to her count what remains.

You drop to the court floor while the last captains still argue, and a loft shadow arrives among them like a delivered parcel. The war court stops arguing. It is very pleased to have something to agree on at last, and what it agrees on is you. The rite gains a witness in irons, and the court keeps the lesson.

You send Sable ahead alone, because she asks with her hands on her hips, and she is back in a hundred breaths with the rite floor chalked on her own palm. Ringers here, cradle there, ranks so, and a posterns seam in the wall that she cannot open but thinks you might. Then her flat advice, delivered eye to eye. Do not be on the floor when the singing starts.

choices.label: Follow her chalk map down to the floor.

nodes.body: You swing wide on the bell shaft rope to skip the last courses, and the rope pays out one fray too far. It parts at the whipping with a sound like a snapped lute string. A ledge breaks the drop, and the climb back up breaks what is left of your pride. The shaft is abandoned to the torch answers gathering above, and the way down is finished for the night.

You surf the shell heap like a hay slide, and for a breath and a half it is glorious. Then the heap arrives at the antechamber floor and you arrive in it, buried to the waist at the feet of the door watch. Many small unfriendly hands dig you out, none of them gentle, and the rite gains a front row audience in irons.

You borrow a sconce torch against the deep dark and carry it like a confession down the boneyard passage. At the first turning, every eye the deep owns answers the light, wide and pale and interested. You douse it far too late to be unseen. The dark that follows is full of soft feet, and the pens are full by floodtide, you among them.

A wardens vestry niche opens off the choir way, gear of the drowned order racked a century deep in silt dust. Rotted cord, green bronze, vestments gone to lace. And one horn of pale wound shell that the dust has not touched at all, hanging apart, as if the years agreed to leave it be. You take it down with a wardens prayer you improvise on the spot, and it settles against your hip like it has been waiting.

choices.label: Carry the wardens shell horn to the blind side.

nodes.body: You join the last rank of the procession at a mourners shuffle, heads down among Reedcloak backs, borrowed gray reeking of bilge water. The column carries you toward the font the way a tide carries driftwood, no effort asked, none offered. Bells walk the pace. Nobody looks at anybody in a rite, which is the one mercy of ceremonies, and the lamp ring glow grows ahead of you stride by stride.

choices.label: Peel off into the pillar shadows at the lamp ring.
Ride the column all the way to the font.

nodes.body: You meet the honor guard captain eye to eye with empty hands shown. A veteran with a rope scarred neck, he listens to three sentences of truth without blinking, which is more than most humans manage. Then he jerks his chin toward Gorse and steps aside. The captain hears mad things himself tonight, he says in trade tongue, and one more can join the queue.

choices.label: Put the truth to Gorse under the captains truce.
Offer the captain silver to clear your way.

nodes.body: You join the relief ringers line at the carry, a hand bell shouldered like a sack of turnips, chin tucked, elbows in. The line trots the gallery stair in step, and the balcony arrives around you from inside the rhythm itself, smoke and thunder and sweating backs. Nobody counts heads in a relief line. The bells do all the counting tonight, and they only count beats.

choices.label: Set the bell down and drift to the far stair.
Keep the bell and play along in the line.
Shoulder a second bell to look busier.

nodes.body: You join your voice to Elowens counter chant on the second phrase, tuneless but stubborn, and two voices make the wrong note bigger than one. The seal glow gutters like a candle in a doorway. The Whispers song tears down the middle, half of it chasing your voice, half clinging to the verse, and holding neither. Elowens hand finds your sleeve in the dark and grips it, and the chant walks forward toward the font.

choices.label: Press through the dying song toward the font.

nodes.body: You kick the burning oil in a bright fan across the woven mats, and flame walks up the embroidered saints at a stroll. Pale robes abandon rank for water and blame in equal measure. The font stands alone in a widening ring of light, unguarded because everything near it is on fire, which is a kind of guarding, but not against you.

choices.label: Take the stone straight off the font in the glare.
Herd the flames wider before you move.

nodes.body: The fire keeps its own counsel. A gust off the door gap folds the flame sheet back across your line, and you come out of it singed and smoked, coughing, in the open. The ranks close around you like a purse, and the rite is delayed one tide by the damage, no more, and the pens keep the party warm while the mats are replaced.

You take the ringers stair against the tide of routing Coil hands, up where everything else is going down. And burst out onto the bell towers broken shoulder with the seal stone against your ribs. The night wind is astonished to see you. Below, the temple empties itself in torch streams from every gate, and the ring wall runs north toward the breach, silver under the setting moon, a road nobody else wants tonight.

choices.label: Run the ring wall north and down the breach.

nodes.body: Bell tower to ring wall to breach, and then the mire at a hunters pace under the setting moon. You loop wide of every Coil line of march for two days, cold camps and stolen samphire and watches shared out in whispers. The stone rides quiet on your back, as if it approves of patience. And on the third dawn the smoke of Barrowmeres chimneys comes up over the downs like a promise kept.

choices.label: Come home the long way.

nodes.body: You pry at the deepest carving for the crown behind it, and the wardens masonry answers exactly as built. A soft fall of counterweighted stone seals the choir way both ways, patient as a mousetrap a century in the setting. Puzzled pale hands dig you out at dawn into custody, the rite long over. Merely postponed, and the wardens, wherever they are, unsurprised.

Silt dust and one held sneeze, and on the ninth landing the sneeze wins its argument. It echoes down the colonnade like a war horn of the nose, impossible to mistake for anything else. The pillar shadows empty of everything but the Coil, and the stair delivers you to the floor by the shortest route, under escort, with your eyes still watering.

The honor guard captain weighs your silver with genuine sorrow. Then he takes it, and you, both. A bribe is property, he explains in careful trade tongue, and property goes to the captain. The queue for Gorses justice is long tonight, and the rite is entirely visible from it, which the Reedcloaks consider a kindness to prisoners with nothing else to watch.

The relief line steps on the offbeat you supplied, and the stumble runs up the whole file bell by bell, a wave of ruined time. The ring master wheels with his tally cord already singing. The balcony becomes a very small room with one door, and the door is not on your side of the room, and neither is anything else.

The third call is one call too many. The panic curdles into pattern, captains triangulating the vaults with torch lines, methodical as surveyors. The call answers its own echo in a room already full of listeners. And the listeners are very interested in meeting the caller, and the meeting is not long delayed.

The second lashing parts with a groan the bells cannot cover, and the scaffold sags like a tired horse. The ring master follows the sound to its source with his cord coiled, unhurried, because the gallery holds everything it catches and it has just caught you. The scaffold, for the record, holds all night.

You widen the postern crack one finger for a better view, and the view improves in both directions. The Whispers eye meets yours down the gap for a heartbeat that lasts a season. Then the blind side is blind no more, and small hard hands are already at the postern seam, and the choir way gives up its last secret along with its guests.

You take the statues front in the open to save a scramble, and your silhouette prints on the lamp glow for the whole floor to read. The descent finishes in a forest of raised poles grown up around the chin. You are helped down the kings nose with more ceremony than kindness, and the rite gains its audience.

You pause to wrap the humming stone mid run, and the hum swells under the cloth like a hive disturbed. Its one note calls down the corridors clear as a bell, to everything that hunts by ear tonight. They run you down within sight of the drain, the stone singing cheerfully in your arms the whole way, loyal to no one.

The bound master is paraded once around the terrace for the freed to see, and one gloating circuit becomes two. His lips move inside the gag on the second lap. The coral sigil at his throat flares white, and the eel line is suddenly holding nothing but warm air and a smell of brine. The night is still won, the people still free, the stone still yours. But the ledger is left open, and somewhere north a debt is walking.

The wardens wayside shrine stands at the town edge, freshly swept by an unknown hand, its stone basin clean and its carved lantern niche empty. Around the basin rim run old strokes worn almost smooth, names maybe, or a rule. Somebody still keeps this place, after two hundred years, and swept it this very morning, and the besom leaning on the basin is still damp.

choices.label: Read the worn strokes with a wet thumb.
Leave a coin and go to the taproom.
Keep the shrine watch past curfew.

nodes.body: You wet a thumb and read the strokes the old way, by touch. Twelve names, worn but orderly, and a thirteenth space left uncut, waiting. Elowen copies the letter forms without a word. Whoever sweeps the shrine never came forward, and the besom stays leaning on the basin, bristles wet, patient as the thirteenth space.

choices.label: Carry the thirteenth space to the inn.

nodes.body: You keep the shrine watch past the curfew bell, and the town gate bars on schedule behind you. The watch admires the gesture and does not bend the rule, and a night on the shrine step buys a stiff neck and a late start that the south road collects with interest. The sweeper, whoever they are, does not come, and the thirteenth space stays uncut.

Pip the pot boy sells you a fen riddle for a copper, delivered with tremendous ceremony. What has a bed and never sleeps, a mouth and never eats. His own answer comes whispered like contraband, the marsh, the marsh. And he pockets the copper with the satisfaction of a boy who has sold the same riddle four times today and considers it honest work.

choices.label: Pay the copper and find Pell.

nodes.body: You study Heddas map scrap flat on the bar while the taproom roars around you. The Greymarsh circle is inked over older, fainter lines, causeway roads from when the fen was fields. And under the circle, in strokes older than any road, one word surfaces where a thumb has worn the ink thin. Choir, and you carry the word to Brams forge like a live coal.

choices.label: Take the word choir to Bram.

nodes.body: Hedda hands up her parting gift at the gate, a tide table copied in her own square hand from the fords ledger, floodtides marked in red for the next seven nights. Her advice comes with it, flat as the table itself. Be done before the seventh, she says, and does not explain, and the cart rolls south with the red marks riding in your breast pocket like a heartbeat.

choices.label: Roll south with the tide table.

nodes.body: A dust plume walks the downs ahead, wagons maybe, riders maybe, too far to say and too placed to ignore. The road will tell its own story in an hour. Running the plume down costs the hour and buys the answer now, and the fen edge does not move while you decide, although it feels like it might.

choices.label: Run the plume down.
Let the road tell it and push on.
Chase it past the second valley.

nodes.body: The plume runs down to a sheep drift and a laughing shepherd, nothing but hooves and dry weather. But the shepherds news is solid as his crook. Gray wagons ran the fen edge road three nights straight, heavy south, light north, and his dogs lay flat and silent for every one of them, which in a lifetime of dogs he has never seen.

choices.label: Bank the news and make the fen edge.

nodes.body: The fen edge road arrives with the shepherds news banked and the afternoon mostly intact. Wagons at night, dogs gone quiet, the crossing planned one fact richer than it would have been. Small coins of knowledge, Elowen says, but the fen makes change in exactly that currency.

choices.label: Join the fen edge road at the wreck.

nodes.body: The plume gets chased across two valleys and runs down to dust and deer, an afternoon spent like a bad coin. The fen edge arrives too late to cross before dark, and the night is spent on the wrong side of the water. Listening to the bell note carry and counting the red marks on the tide table by firelight, one night poorer.

The weir walk runs below Wecks landing, hurdles and plank ways over sliding black water, a shortcut worth half the afternoon. Weck names his toll without shame. One hours honest work lifting eel traps, each one heavy as sin and twice as lively. The fog is rising off the river like a slow curtain, and the plank line runs away into it, slick and inviting and unpaid for.

choices.label: Pay the hour and lift traps.
Walk the plank line fast through the fog.

nodes.body: The plank line in fog is a lesson in humility. One slick board turns underfoot, and the weir pool takes you, cold as charity. Eel men haul you out and wring you out on the bank, kind and unhurried and full of advice. The day drains away west with the trail in it, and the company sleeps wet in Wecks boat shed, one shortcut poorer.

Traps lifted and toll paid, Wecks tongue loosens with the shared work. A Coil tithe barge poled upriver three nights back, he says, riding heavy with sealed jars, gray hands poling in step like soldiers. And the barge that followed it, a cousins, curious, never came back down. He spits into the weir pool and waits to see what you make of it.

choices.label: Ask what the Coil fears about water.

nodes.body: Wecks shrug is worth a chapter. The Coil hates open water, he says, hates it like cats do, and pays for river freight anyway. So whatever rides in those sealed jars matters more than the hate, and it is all going one direction, toward the marsh country, while every eye in the district watches the hills out of habit. Follow the crows, he says. The crows have it right.

choices.label: File it away and follow the crows west.

nodes.body: A bell note drifts over the carrlands where no church stands, thin and wrong and patient, one tone that starts nowhere and refuses to finish. The droves themselves bend away from it, you notice, the old lanes curving wide like grass leaning from a fire. Something in the fen rings, and the cattle roads have known it longer than the maps have.

choices.label: Follow the bell note to the rise.
Bend away from it like the droves do.
Send one scout toward it, no more.

nodes.body: The note leads to a rise, and below the rise a fen funeral poles past in silence, mourners in undyed wool, a coffin boat riding low. And behind the mourners, unmourning, two outriders in gray watch the watchers. Faces turned not to the dead but to the hedges and the rises, to exactly the places someone like you would stand. The Coil attends even grief now, taking attendance.

choices.label: Withdraw the long quiet way.

nodes.body: You leave the rise the long quiet way, hedgerow to hedgerow, and the funeral poles on unbothered. The gray outriders never turn your way, which is either luck or the width of the detour. The fen edge road arrives by dusk with the lesson filed where lessons keep. The Coil watches grief, and counts its watchers, and the fen buries its dead under audit.

choices.label: Make the fen edge road by dusk.

nodes.body: You watch the funeral one rise too near, and the gray outriders fold around the watchers with the peculiar gentleness kept for witnesses. The droves end at a picket post with your kit inventoried and your names requested politely. And the fen buries its dead without you while you learn how much the Coil pays for curiosity. It pays in pole rides south.

The fen mothers sewing kit lies spilled at the wreck, pins and thread and a bone thimble, and among them a lodestone needle hung on a twist of flax. A poor womans compass, fen made, still pointing true when everything else at this wreck points wrong. It is small and exact and someone loved it, and the marsh ahead is a country where sky and water trade places without warning.

choices.label: Pocket the lodestone needle.
Linger sorting the whole kit.

nodes.body: The sewing kit holds you one sort too long, and the reeds move twice while you kneel. A search party of gray hoods beats back along the bank on its own schedule, unhurried, thorough, and the wreck holds its visitors for them like a shopkeeper holding a parcel. Needle and all, the company is collected, and the mud takes new prints, all pointing south.

The needle wraps in its own flax and goes into the safest pocket you own. In the deep mire, Reed says, fog comes down until up is a rumor, and a needle that points home is worth more than iron. You check the reed bank one more time on the way out, and the wreck watches you go with its broken mouth open.

choices.label: Check the reed bank and move on.

nodes.body: You share your own ration out with Reed while his color comes back, bread and smoked eel split fen fashion, biggest piece to the hurt. He watches that arithmetic happen and something in him settles. The marsh calls get taught over the shared bread, heron, plover, all clear, kin teaching kin, and the road south picks up one guide and loses all its guesswork.

choices.label: Walk Reed to Saltmire hamlet.

nodes.body: The headwomans story comes by the peat fire, told to the flames. Her grandmother dove the temple grounds as a girl on a dare, down through the brown dark to the drowned door itself. And the door hummed against her palm like a hive, she said, warm in the cold water, and she kicked for the light and never told the story sober. The fire spits, and nobody interrupts.

choices.label: Hear the story to its end.

nodes.body: The story ends with the grandmothers rule, three words delivered like law. Never at floodtide. She would not say why, the headwoman says, only that the hum was different those nights, eager, and the fire spits while everyone at it pretends not to count the red marks on your tide table. Five nights, the arithmetic says, and stops being arithmetic.

choices.label: Count the nights and pack.

nodes.body: You check the tide table against the grandmothers rule by firelight, and the sums pack the kit by themselves. Five nights before the big flood, four of travel if the fen is kind, which it is not. The margin is a day, maybe less. The headwoman watches you fold the table away and nods once, like a woman watching someone finally read the weather she has smelled all week.

choices.label: Take the trail with the arithmetic done.

nodes.body: The crannog dog swims out to meet the party a mile early, gray muzzled and half wild with waiting, and will not be called off. It leads the last stretch by the dry line only a dog could know, tail up, certain. Checking over its shoulder that the slow two legged rescue is still coming. Col is standing on the crannog boards before you see him, watching the dog bring you in like a tide he ordered personally.

choices.label: Follow the dog to the crannog.

nodes.body: Pale lights swing off the causeways south side, lantern soft, swaying like carried lamps going somewhere reasonable, a line of them walking out across the open mire. Reeds hand closes on your sleeve before anyone steps toward them. Those are not lamps, he says, flat. Nobody carries a lamp where there is nothing to stand on, and nothing is exactly what is out there.

choices.label: Wait the lights out in the parapet shadow.
Follow the lights for a closer look.

nodes.body: The lights get followed three stones too far, for a closer look that never gets close. The causeway edge arrives mid stride where no edge showed, and the black water closes over the lights last joke without a ripple. The remaining lights gutter out one by one, unhurried, and the mire smooths itself flat again. Patient as ever, already setting the table for the next travelers.

You wait the lights out in the parapet shadow, and they tire the way liars do, drifting apart, guttering, sulking out one by one until the mire is only dark again. The causeway ahead stands empty and honest. Reed lets go of your sleeve, and nobody mentions the one light that guttered last, the one that had gotten so close.

choices.label: Take the empty causeway on.

nodes.body: The last dry mile goes with eyes front and the lights returning behind you, dancing where you would have drowned, patient as anglers. The ring wall rises ahead, broken and torch lit and made by hands, which tonight feels almost friendly by comparison. Whatever hunts with lamps in the open mire stays in the open mire, and the temple has only people in it.

choices.label: Come up on the ring wall.

nodes.body: A sea eagles eyrie crowns the highest esker, a nest the size of a cart bed on a leaning stack. And the great bird rides its updraft overhead like a posted notice. The crest path passes directly beneath. Reed reads the sky the way Sable reads locks, and names the hunting hour, when the bird works the far meres and the nest stands empty of everything but appetite.

choices.label: Pass beneath in the hunting hour.
Pass at midday under the birds eye.

nodes.body: The eyrie gets passed at the wrong hour, and the great bird stoops once in warning and once in earnest. The crest path surrenders at a run, packs shielding heads, and the rations bag pays the toll, carried off over the meres in two great unhurried wingbeats. The esker road resumes, poorer and politer, and supper is samphire.

The eyrie passes at a crouch in the birds hunting hour, the nest looted of nothing, dignity intact, one white splashed rock at a time. Far off over the meres the eagle stoops on something that is not you, which is all anyone asked of the arrangement. The watch platform shows below by its smoke, and the crest path runs on toward it.

choices.label: Drop toward the watch platform.

nodes.body: The sluice junction where four mouths meet is an echo chamber built by accident, every word returned fourfold with interest. The drain plays a counting game with sound here, and plays it better than visitors do. Reed snaps his fingers once and the dark answers four times from four directions, none of them quite honest.

choices.label: Map the echoes with finger snaps.

nodes.body: You map the echoes with finger snaps, patient as surveying. Each mouth answers at its own delay, near, far, farther, and one that swallows sound instead of returning it, which Reed says is the flooded one. The true way sorts itself from the liars by ear alone, second mouth, short echo, dry floor. The lantern never even comes out of its hood.

choices.label: Trust the snapped map and go.
Play one more round to be sure.

nodes.body: The echo game plays one round too clever. And the four mouths sing your position in chorus to ears that know the chamber better than any visitor. Lanterns arrive on the true way first, because the true way is theirs. And the junction delivers the company upward into custody with the argument about cleverness settled for good.

The snapped map holds true. The second mouth runs dry and straight, and the cistern ladder arrives exactly where the short echo promised it. Behind you the junction goes on talking to itself in four voices, arguing about where you went. And the drain delivers you up toward the terrace with the argument unresolved.

choices.label: Climb out at the cistern ladder.

nodes.body: Old Mag the kiln tender sits at her banked fire, blind these ten years and missing nothing. She names each of you by boot sound before anyone speaks, dwarf, light girl, scholar, and you, the one who walks like a decision. Tea happens whether wanted or not. Then terms, because nothing crosses Mags galleries free, and she has been waiting all season for someone worth charging.

choices.label: Take Mags tea and terms.
Haggle the blind woman down.

nodes.body: You haggle the blind woman down one measure too far, and Mags face closes like a ledger. Her char gets carried by someone else this season, and her directions go with it. The company backtracks unguided through galleries that all agree with Mag, and the night spends itself in dead ends while the tide table burns another red mark.

Mags terms are honest. Carry her season of char to the head of the straight run, four back loads of it, and in trade her directions, exact to the prop and true as a plumb line. The char gets carried, the shoulders complain, and her last direction comes free at the gallery mouth. When the bells go quick, she says, be above ground or be still.

choices.label: Carry the char and take her directions.

nodes.body: The pen grate comes within reach along the wall shadow, and Cols mother grips the bars with both hands. Names and numbers pass fast and quiet, eleven alive, Marta leading, the child Whin coughing but strong. And the guard fires wander closer down the wall with every traded breath, patient, on their round, ending the conversation one circuit at a time.

choices.label: Break off and slip the midden door.
Trade one more breath of names.

nodes.body: One traded breath too many at the grating, and a guard fire wanders close enough to light both faces. The grate watch collects the visitor its prisoners tried to wave away, gently, with poles, and Cols mother watches you marched off with the names still warm between you. The pens gain one more tenant who knew better, and the fires resume their round.

The pens whisper relay works the way Marta built it, word passed cell to cell in the quiet game rhythm. Tap and pause and tap, an inventory of the living taken through stone. Eleven answers come back down the line, each one a name and a heartbeat, and the last tap settles into silence like a coin into a well.

choices.label: Pass the word down the relay.

nodes.body: The relay finishes at the last cell, eleven counted twice over, none missing, none broken past mending. Then the warder stirs in his chair as the last tap settles, smacking his lips, resettling, and the whole pens hold their breath together in a silence so practiced it has calluses. The snore resumes. The night resumes with it.

choices.label: Start the freeing at the key board.
Tap one more question down the line.

nodes.body: The whisper relay plays too loud at the last cell, the quiet game lost by a syllable, and the warder rises from his chair with the key board already swinging. Old ears in an old room. The pens hold their breath and it does not matter, because the count he takes is of shadows, and the shadows come up four too many.

The temples own history stands stacked in the vault as loot, carved panels pried whole from the choir, drowned saints with their faces tallied for sale in chalk. The lots are marked for a buyer in the mountains, a peak over a coil drawn on every third crate. The Coil is not just raising a king. It is selling the house he sleeps in, room by room, to somebody higher up.

choices.label: Copy the buyers mark and move.
Read the lots into the shift hours.

nodes.body: The history lots read lot by lot into the small hours of the shift, panel after tallied panel. And the vault door closes on schedule with the reader still inside. Morning delivers the inventory, four strangers found among the stolen past, priced and processed with everything else. The buyer in the mountains, whoever they are, nearly acquired a matched set.

The buyers mark copies quick off three lots, a peak over a coil, drawn small into Elowens case beside the warden marks. The panels stay as found, history too heavy to rescue tonight, and the vault door eases shut on all those tallied faces. Somebody in the mountains is buying a drowned religion by the crate, and now there is proof of the address.

choices.label: Take the service stair with the mark.

nodes.body: A dead ward shell lies on the grotto floor, cracked and silent, its spiral gone gray. Safe to handle, Elowen judges, turning it with a chalk stick first. Above, the live wards on their gut lines chatter softly at their fallen sister, a sound like distant gossip, and the dead shell weighs nothing at all in the hand, like a promise already kept.

choices.label: Pocket the dead shell for Elowen.
Flip it once in triumph first.

nodes.body: The dead shell gets flipped once in triumph and clicks against a live line, and the chatter spreads ward to ward like gossip at a wedding. The grotto closes around its visitors while the song never breaks, never hurries. And the last thing the flooded margin offers is the observation that in this temple, even the dead things testify.

The dead shell goes into Elowens case for later, a wards whole anatomy in one cracked spiral, the first piece of Coil craft anyone has ever carried out for study. She wraps it like an egg and looks pleased in the way she gets pleased, quietly and completely. The den glow waits ahead through the incense, and the song goes on unbothered.

choices.label: Slip on toward the den glow.

nodes.body: Marta raises her right hand in the rush light and deputizes the entire pen under peat law. Cutters, children, and you, sworn carriers of one consignment, namely each other. The oath is older than marshals and twice as binding, she says, and nobody laughs, because eleven backs straighten under it like masts taking sail. You sign the bond with your mark, and the pen becomes a crew with a cargo.

choices.label: Set the signal and go to the gallery.

nodes.body: Korvanes camp desk stands in the lattice gaps narrow slice of view, and on it, beside the chained folio, lies an open ledger in a fine hand. Sums paid to the Reedcloaks. Sums owed to the mountain seat, name after name after name. And one entry underlined at the foot of the page, in fresher ink. The seat expects the second door open by winter, and payment on the third seal. You carry the words down the stairs like a hot coal.

choices.label: Carry the ledger line down the stairs.

nodes.body: Murals line the choir way where the sound slots pause. The twelve wardens stand painted at this same door in older robes, twelve singers with one raised hand. The door is shown shut, and behind it the crowned sleeper dreams a flood that fills the next three panels, brown and total, herds drowned in the furrows, towns gone to reed. You take the lesson with you along the passage. The door was never a treasure house, only a lid.

choices.label: Take the lesson to the doors blind side.

nodes.body: The pillar forest floor offers its own road, bench stones and offering troughs giving belly cover between bell beats. You go down among the rotundas own furniture and crawl, elbow and knee, while rank boots pass an arms length away. Silt of centuries packs your sleeves. The bells cover everything except your own heart, which sounds, from down here, like somebody knocking to be let out.

choices.label: Crawl the last trough line to the lamp ring.
Crawl through the bell pause to save time.

nodes.body: The trough line ends in the cradles own shadow, silt in your teeth and bells overhead like weather. The waking words begin close enough to feel on the back of your neck, dry syllables settling like frost. You gather your legs under you in the blind side dark and count the singers breaths, one, two, and rise.

choices.label: Rise on the blind side and move on the rite.

nodes.body: You are one trough short of the shadow when the bells pause between verses. In the sudden hush your crawl sounds like a landslide. Small hard hands find your ankles before the next peal lands, and the trough line ends where the cradles lamp ring begins, with you presented silt gray and blinking to the rite you nearly reached.

The inn stable holds Tallows cart ponies and one extra offer, a borrowed mare from a drover heading north, sound of wind but shy of water, he admits. The stable boy grooms her while wearing the expression of a witness under oath. She is fast, he allows, and on roads. The fen, he does not say, is not roads, and the mare watches the south gate like it owes her money.

choices.label: Decline the mare and keep the cart.
Take the borrowed mare south.

nodes.body: Pell delivers his last warning over the bar rag, quiet, once. The fen took his brother whole in his fathers day, cart and team and all, between one blink and the next. The family kept one rule after, and he gives it to you the way you hand over a knife, handle first. Never argue with ground that moves. Then he goes back to polishing, and the subject is closed forever.

choices.label: Carry the rule to Bram at his wagon.

nodes.body: The borrowed mare bolts at the first shivering ground, which is a mile before the fen even starts trying. Kit scatters down a wet mile of drove. And the day spends itself catching her and apologizing to the drover instead of making the fen edge. She watches you repack from behind the stable boy, unrepentant, and the south road starts over tomorrow.

You correct the braggarts version loudly, with names and particulars, and half the taproom takes notes. By morning the companys route, strength, and departure hour have run south ahead of it to ears that pay for such things. The fen edge greets you with a picket that knew you were coming, and the tuition for the lesson is the whole expedition.

You study the drowned bell tower from the crest a long moment, shoulder deep in its own parish, weathercock still true. And once, clear across the whole shining mire, its bell swings with no hand on the rope. One note, round and patient, and then still, and nobody says anything. The fen edge road waits below, and the note goes down the path with you, riding in the space between heartbeats.

choices.label: Go down to the fen edge with the bells note.

nodes.body: You walk the bank while Weck ties up, and the mud offers a tithe jar shard, gray sealed, snake stamped, edges fresh. You sniff it once and throw it hard away, brine and something under the brine, sweet and old and wrong. Weck watches the shard fly and nods slowly, like a man having a suspicion confirmed that he had hoped to keep as a suspicion.

choices.label: Rejoin Weck at the stage.

nodes.body: You read the ford marker stone close, older than the droves it serves, and under the moss a warden mark surfaces, an arrow pointing south and two words in the old strokes. Choir sleeps. Elowen copies it twice and stares at the second copy. Whoever marked the fords knew what was down there, she says, and wanted travelers reminded whose bedroom the road passes.

choices.label: Cross at the marker and push on.

nodes.body: You cross the ford fast without sounding it, and the drowned second step is deeper than any yesterday. The kit swims. You swim after it. The days progress ends at a fire on the wrong bank, drying fifty crowns of gear one sock at a time, and the marker stone watches with the patience of an object that has seen this exact evening a thousand times.

A childs slate turns up in the wreck straw, letters practiced in a careful hand. Mama, boat, home. The words sit on the slate the way small stones sit on a grave, and the reed bank the crows shun becomes, suddenly, the only thing in the world worth checking. You are moving before anyone says so, slate still in hand.

choices.label: Check the reed bank now.

nodes.body: The council hour goes flat on the crannog boards over the map, lantern hooded to a coin of light. Sable argues for the sluice, dark and dry. Bram argues the causeway like an honest soldier, walkable and fast. Then Elowen asks what the wardens themselves would have used, and everyone stops, and looks at the drains again, and the map is quietly redrawn around a better question.

choices.label: Sleep on it and let the tide choose.

nodes.body: You push the trail at moonrise against every counsel given, and misread the night sign within the mile. The picket that watches the drag trail by dark folds around the party like a purse string drawing shut. The rescue joins the rescued, poled south under a senseless moon, and the mire keeps the lesson for whoever comes next, which now must be nobody.

A patrol horn lies dropped on the causeway stones, dice cup beside it, and its owners are gaming instead of walking their beat, hunched over the throws in a parapet gap. Their backs are to the narrows for one more round, and one more after that, the way dice always promise. The narrows stand open behind them, exactly as wide as their bad habits.

choices.label: Walk the narrows behind their backs.
Watch one more throw from cover.

nodes.body: You walk the narrows behind the gamers backs, unhurried, while the dice click their little cover rhythm. Somebody below is losing a weeks pay with tremendous feeling. The ring wall gains four shadows it never counted, and the game, as far as anyone ever learns, is the only thing that happened on the causeway tonight.

choices.label: Push on to the ring wall.

nodes.body: You watch one throw too many from too near, and a gamers eye catches the extra shadow at the parapet. The pair proves they can do their job after all, loudly, with the horn, and the causeway wakes end to end. The narrows close, the dice get packed away. And the company is poled to the temple by two sentries suddenly very intent on looking industrious.

A pilgrims cache stone sits on the crest with its lid ajar, oilcloth and hard bread inside still sound, left by hands that kept the old ways. And coiled on top, gray with years, an old climbing rope, laid up when the stair was young. The bread is honest, and the oilcloth is welcome, and the rope is a question with strands in it.

choices.label: Take the oilcloth and bread, leave the rope.
Coil the old rope onto your pack.

nodes.body: The cache rope goes onto the pack against the strands advice, and gets trusted on the next scarp. Gray fibers part at the first honest load, quietly, without drama, the way old things fail. The scarp foot collects what the years already paid for, and the pilgrims cache keeps its bread and its lesson both, one of them still edible.

An otter run wears smooth beside the seized dam, a body wide slide under the works, polished by generations of belly fur. It is the otters own door into the lower drain, and it will pass one person at a time, going like an otter, arms first, faith second. Reed looks at it with professional respect. The otters, he says, have never once been caught.

choices.label: Go like an otter, kit dragged behind.
Send the kit through and follow blind.

nodes.body: The otter run accepts a wardens shoulders exactly halfway, then files a complaint. Stuck fast at the turn with water rising to argue about it, you spend an hour of backward inches buying your own body back from the stone. The nights margin spends itself whole in that hour, and the otters, wherever they are, say nothing.

The diggers work song comes through a props width of peat, call and answer, older than the Coils silver and borrowed by it. You learn the answer line through the wall, humming it under breath until it sits in the mouth ready. A crew that sings together counts together, Mag had said, and a stranger who knows the answer line is not a stranger, most nights.

choices.label: Hum the answer line and pass as crew.
Sing out on the next call to be sure.

nodes.body: You sing out on the next call to be sure, and you are one call early. The work song stops dead, mid beat, the way songs stop when everyone singing them knows everyone else. Tallow lights converge on the singer who knew the words but not the timing, and the galleries close their one door with the singing, for tonight, concluded.

The warders water round comes due by the tide clock, and a jug filled at the brine vats gets carried to his chair on schedule by someone in a borrowed gray hood, head down, steps even. Routine is the best disguise in any prison. The jug lands by his boot on the clocks minute, and the snore never changes key.

choices.label: Deliver the round on the clocks minute.
Deliver it early and be done.

nodes.body: You deliver the water round a half turn early, and the warder wakes to a stranger in gray pouring his jug. Old chair, old reflexes. His hand has your wrist before his eyes have a face, and his shout has the wing before your excuse has a verb. The round was right, and the minute was wrong, and the pens collect the difference.

The hall hound circles the gallery screen on its rounds, old and honest and doing its work. One strip of dried eel offers itself flat palmed through the carving, fen manners, no sudden anything. The hound considers the offer, and the offerer, with eyes that have seen every trick a feast hall breeds. And takes the eel with a gravity that closes the negotiation.

choices.label: Feed it flat palmed and pass on.
Pull back the strip when it snuffles.

nodes.body: The strip pulls back when the hound snuffles, one flinch of doubt, and the hound takes the eel and the wrist behind it, gentle as a vice. An honest sentry doing honest work. Its baying brings the benches before the grip can be argued with, and the feast collects its intruders with the hound accepting congratulations throughout.

A novice lies awake in the last cell, young and scared and watching you cross her doorway, and the night balances on a held breath. Her hand is a span from the bell rope. Yours is a span from nothing useful. What passes between you is not quite a bargain and not quite mercy, one long look, and then her eyes close, deliberately, and stay closed while you pass.

choices.label: Hold her eyes and back away slow.
Lunge to hush her first.

nodes.body: You lunge to hush her first, and her nerve breaks toward the bell rope, which was always going to win the race. Her scream and the peal arrive together. The dormitory wing rises around its youngest and proudest in the same breath, and the grotto files its guests where the Coil files everything, behind iron, with the novice watching you go, unblinking.

You fall in behind a train of plunder porters crossing the emptying court, a crate on your shoulder for a pass and your chin tucked for a face. Korvanes own baggage carries you toward the deep stair in plain sight, which is the joke of it. The best shadows in the temple turn out to be other peoples burdens. You set the crate down at the stair head and step sideways into the dark.

choices.label: Set the crate down at the stair head.

nodes.body: A wardens sound slot sits at the posterns own hinge, and it frames the font close enough to count the seal stones rivets. You learn the Whispers song breath by breath from here, the rise, the held note, the little falling turn. And you mark the exact half beat where the singer must inhale, a door left open in the music, wide enough for one moving shadow.

choices.label: Move on the rite at the half beat.

nodes.body: You count the lamp ring from the last pillar. Eleven lamps, two guttering, and the gap between the guttering pair is the width of a coffin boat. You rehearse the routes to the cradle twice behind closed eyes, every stride assigned its bell beat, before a single boot moves. Then the near lamp dips, the gap opens, and the rehearsal becomes the performance.

choices.label: Move on the rite through the guttering gap.
Cross while the near lamp gutters lowest.

nodes.body: One guttering lamp chooses that exact heartbeat to flare. The gap becomes a stage, and the crawl becomes a performance, reviewed at pole point by the nearest rank. The rite pauses politely while you are collected off the floor and dusted. And then resumes, because nothing about you delays a door that has waited this long.

You misjudge Gorses patience by one demand too many, and the captain grows tired of humans who bargain like victors. His hand moves a fingers width. The honor guard closes with the peculiar gentleness hired men save for valuable hostages. And you spend the rite in the captains own keeping, an asset now, awaiting valuation.

Your elbow splits the bell skin with one misjudged shift, and the flat note sits in the wall of right ones like a broken tooth. The ring masters cord finds the flaw faster than thought can excuse it. The gallery box shuts its one door, and the night finishes without you in it, rung out in every sense there is.

Old Maren beckons from her corner table and asks for the brass lantern itself, lit and set between you. Her milky eyes catch the flame the way younger eyes catch print, and the taproom noise bends politely around her table without touching it. She reads a long time. The flame stands still for her in a room full of drafts, which everyone pretends is normal.

choices.label: Set the lit lantern between you.

nodes.body: The reading comes flat and slow, like tide arriving. The lantern remembers a door, she says, and it will remember another. When the flame leans with no draft, follow the lean. Then she pushes the lantern back across the table and is done with you entirely, calling for her small beer, and the flame, you notice, leans very slightly south the whole way back to your table.

choices.label: Take the reading to Bram at his wagon.

nodes.body: The wrecked barges false bottom answers a knuckle tap, and a smugglers board lifts on the familys real cargo. Not contraband. Books, wrapped dry in oilcloth, and a parish register older than the drowned chapel, the fen villages whole memory sent away for safekeeping. The Coil took the family and missed the freight, and the freight is the reason the family mattered.

choices.label: Hide the register better and note the place.

nodes.body: The register wraps back watertight and hides better than the smugglers managed. Deep in the bank willows, and Elowen notes the place in her case with a scholars exactness. Whoever lives to tell the family will know where their memory sleeps. It is a small stubborn insurance against the worst, and the reed bank is calling, and you go.

choices.label: Go to the reed bank the crows shun.

nodes.body: You ask Weck plainly about the charm he hums at the weirs, and he gives it plainly. His grandmothers weir song for keeping fingers when the eels run heavy. Four verses of sense, and one verse nobody in the family ever understood, about a door that hums back if you sing it right. His grandmother had it from the drowned parish, he says, and never once sang that verse after dark.

choices.label: Carry the weir song to the fen edge.

nodes.body: You read the hedge bones by lantern shade, and they are not hedge at all. Post holes in a great ring, the bones of an old droving corral from when the carrlands ran cattle, and where drovers penned herds there is always water. The well sits sweet under its lid ten paces on, and every waterskin fills to bursting while the storm grumbles itself out. And the camp rises watered and rested for the trail.

choices.label: Rise watered and rested for the trail.

nodes.body: A Coil polers satchel hangs forgotten on the burned platforms rail, fish hooks and loaded dice and one folded sheet of soft leather. A tide table, scratched in a working hand, floodtides marked in red to a seventh night circled twice. Heddas table says the same nights. The Coil is working to the same deadline you are, and now you know they know it.

choices.label: Pocket the tide table and see to Col.

nodes.body: A chain locker hides in the ring wall footing, portcullis gear a century seized into one red mass. But through the rusted grating a crawl space runs clean under the water gate, and its floor glitters faintly. Dropped coins, generations of them, fallen through the boards above into the one place nobody could ever reach. Until somebody small enough, and poor enough, and here.

choices.label: Crawl the coin floor under the gate.

nodes.body: The crawl space gives onto the winch room stair inside the wall, and your pockets ride heavier by a fistful of strangers coins, old mintings and older. The steps were built for chains to run on, smooth and shallow, and boots go quiet down them as if the stair approves of stealth. Below, the terrace firelight flickers through the door seams, close now.

choices.label: Take the winch stair down into the terrace.

nodes.body: An old beacon basket hangs on the esker crown, iron rusted to black lace, but the bracket bolts still bite true stone. From its ledge a wardens sight line runs straight down the mire, and every approach to the temple lies laid out below like a supper table. Watch fires, gate lamps, the breach, all of it plotted in one long look from a dead orders chair.

choices.label: Fix the watch fires positions from up here.

nodes.body: You commit the watch fires to memory with their blind lanes between, a map made of dark stripes on dark water. Two fires overlap at the breach, but their overlap wanders on the wind, and the wandering has a rhythm. You climb back down to the path knowing exactly where not to be seen, which is most of what stealth ever is.

choices.label: Slip down on the counted blind lanes.

nodes.body: A tally board hangs at the sluice junction, grange marks a century old with Coil chalk over them. Jars in, jars out, a feeding schedule kept in two hands across two ages. And the jars out column stops dead three tides ago, mid count, like a sentence broken off. Whatever the jars fed below has stopped being fed, and nobody chalked why.

choices.label: Carry the tally to the cistern ladder.

nodes.body: You carry the tally like a lamp the rest of the drain. Whatever the jars fed is no longer being fed, which is either mercy or appetite, and drains have never once meant mercy. The cistern ladder arrives with the arithmetic still open, and everyone climbs a little faster than the rungs strictly require.

choices.label: Climb to the terrace grating.

nodes.body: The great mother kiln squats at the gallery head, cold a generation, big enough to walk through upright, the grandmother of every kiln on the line. And its flue runs clean up through the moss into open air behind the ring wall, a chimney road, black as guilt and exactly wide enough. Mag said her mother fired it last. Nothing has burned in it since but rumor.

choices.label: Climb the mother kilns flue.

nodes.body: The flue climbs on knees and back, soot to the eyebrows, breath rationed in the black. The grate at the top lifts on old hinges into terrace shadow, behind the ring wall, inside everything. You come out of the ground like the hills own opinion, filthy and delivered, strength traded fair for surprise, and the grate settles back with nobody the wiser.

choices.label: Lift the grate onto terrace shadow.

nodes.body: The dig crews manifest sits under a stone on the tally shack step, wages owed against names, half the names struck through with one word after each. Diver. Hired hands, paid off the books, converted to the pens when their shifts ended. The Coil does not lay off its labor. It files it below, and the manifest is the filing.

choices.label: Pocket the manifest and make the wall.

nodes.body: The stair walls carry a garrisons worth of scratched boredom, tallies and initials and one rude drawing of a ring master. And among them, cut deeper and older than all of it, a warden mark with an arrow pointing down and two words in the old strokes. Choir stair. The building remembers its keepers, even if nothing else does, and it is still giving directions.

choices.label: Follow the wardens arrow down.

nodes.body: Marta gives you her whispered muster through the grate, the count she takes twice daily to keep heads counted and hearts up. Eleven names in order of age, Whin the youngest at nine and fierce. Two fevered but walking. And Cols mother alive, her arm splinted in reed and profanity. You tell Marta her freight is about to move, and the gray face behind the bars becomes, briefly, a much younger womans.

choices.label: Tell Marta her freight is about to move.

nodes.body: The word passes cell to cell in the quiet game rhythm, tap and pause and tap, and the pens answer it with a silence that has weight. Eleven people pack nothing at all with tremendous care, straw smoothed, cloaks rolled, Whins slate wiped clean. By the time the keys come off the board the pens are ready, and have been ready, you understand, since the first night the diving started.

choices.label: Set the plan with the pen ready.

nodes.body: Elowen reads the Coil chalk against the old script and finds the cheat inside an hour. They are not translating the liturgy, she whispers, they are inverting it. Every hush made a waking, every stay made a rise, the wardens own lullaby turned backward line by line to sing a sleeper up instead of down. The counter, she says, going pale at her own arithmetic, is just to read the old lines as written.

choices.label: Copy the inversion key small.

nodes.body: The inversion key copies small into Elowens chalk case, the old lines and their backward twins side by side. The counter chant is no longer a theory. It is a page, in her case, in her hand, and the court gallery stairs climb ahead with one more weapon in the company than the Coil has counted.

choices.label: Climb to the choir loft lattice.

nodes.body: Two novices gossip past your alcove, robes whispering. The Whisper quarreled with the master again, over wages of all things. She sings for the Coil but she is owed, says the younger. And owed singers, says the older one, flat as a tide table, sing flat when it matters. Their footsteps fade toward the dormitory, and the gossip stays behind, worth its weight.

choices.label: Bank the quarrel and keep moving.

nodes.body: You bank the quarrel like coin. A singer who feels cheated stands between Korvane and his door, the one voice the whole rite cannot do without, and she is counting her wages. The court gallery stairs climb ahead, and the company climbs them carrying one more lever than it came with, small and human and possibly the best one yet.

choices.label: Climb to the loft with the lever.

nodes.body: You count Korvanes pacing from the loft like a jailers rounds. Eleven strides, turn. Eleven strides, turn. A clerks discipline stretched over a debtors nerves, and every eleventh stride his eyes flick to the deep stair, as if something might come up it early, asking after schedules. Whatever Korvane is, he is not the top of his own ladder. He is afraid of the next rung, and that is worth knowing tonight.

choices.label: Slip down to the deep approaches.

nodes.body: You study the last mural panel one breath longer, and the breath pays. Twelve wardens stand at the door, but one is painted turned away, hand over mouth. And under the flaking wash a scribed correction runs in the same old strokes. Eleven sang, and one listened, and the listener keeps the count. You carry that down the choir way toward the blind side, and resolve to be the twelfth.

choices.label: Go to the blind side and keep the count yourself.

nodes.body: You shop the panic like a market day. A dropped torch collected here, a watchword learned from a shouting captain there. Pole racks passed at a porters trudge and one gray robe lifted off its hook in passing. The confusion pays wages to anyone calm enough to collect them, and you walk toward the rite dressed in borrowed chaos, richer by every stride.

choices.label: Move on the rite dressed in borrowed chaos.

nodes.body: You count the rite time from inside the bell line, shoulder to shoulder with thunder. Nine peals and a rest, and nine and a rest. And in every rest the Whispers song stands alone and naked for half a breath, the exact seam in the night where a wrong note would tear the whole cloth. You carry the count down toward the floor like a key carried to its lock.

choices.label: Carry the count down to the rite.
